EPISODE_NARRATIVE: This longer-duration winter event was really made up of 3 separate systems. The first wave brought snow and mixed precipitation to Central Montana on Friday, February 2. After a lull, another round of mostly snow impacted Central Montana on Saturday, February 3. The third, and final, wave brought snow and mixed precipitation to Central and Southwest Montana Sunday-Monday, February 4-5.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Reported 8.6 inches of snow in the Havre area in 24 hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the 7th through 9th, North-Central and Southwest Montana resided underneath northwesterly flow aloft associated with the polar front jet stream. In addition, multiple disturbances embedded within this flow interacted with a frontal boundary that wavered over the area from southwest to northeast and vice versa. The resulting lift, combined with abundant moisture in the low- to mid-level atmosphere, contributed to the formation of periods of snow over North-Central and Southwest Montana. In addition, it is believed mesoscale bands of heavy snow that affected portions of North-Central Montana on the 8th into early morning of the 9th were tied to frontogenesis in the 850 mb to 700 mb layer.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Montana DOT reported severe driving conditions along MT-66 from the junction with US-191 to Hays.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Highway 212 was closed for nearly four hours during the afternoon and evening on February 5th due to a fatal crash three miles west of Joliet. According to Montana Highway Patrol, a man was driving southbound and attempted to pass a pickup in front of him, but with whiteout conditions created from a plow further ahead, he could not see a car coming from the opposite direction. This caused a chain-reaction crash.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low- to mid-level winds (i.e. 700 mb winds) increased and became more westerly across the Rocky Mountain Front during the evening of February 12th through much of the 13th as high pressure ridging over western Montana and the Pacific Northwest was broken-down by a series of weather disturbances. At the surface, a trough east of the Rockies developed and deepened. A tightening surface pressure gradient accompanying this strengthening trough yielded strong and gusty southwesterly to westerly surface winds along the Rocky Mountain Front and most of Glacier County. These strong winds, combined with a significant and loose snow pack, allowed a ground blizzard to affect much of Glacier County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Spotter reported blizzard conditions and very difficult travel conditions due to major blowing and drifting snow in Babb. Peak gust of 81 mph was recorded at 16:20 MST Feb 13th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: After a relatively mild January and first few days of February, an arctic air mass came over the Continental Divide into Northwest Montana during the morning of February 3 bringing strong winds, heavy snow, and localized freezing rain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An arctic air-mass moved over the Continental Divide into West-Central Montana during the evening of the 3rd, leading to snow, blowing snow, and icy roads. Wind gusts of 35 to 45 miles per hour combined with falling snow led to low visibility and drifting snow.  Widespread snow amounts of 3 to 6 inches were measured with isolated totals to 12 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another push of arctic air came back over the Continental Divide the evening of the 4th, reaching Kalispell during the morning commute on the 5th. Winds were not as strong along U.S. Highway 2 as the previous day but were stronger in Kalispell.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe driving conditions occurred on U.S. Highway 2 from late on the 4th to early on the 5th due to a combination of freezing rain, heavy snow, blowing snow, and icy roads. Snow amounts of 4 to 8 inches were common with wind gusts to 35 miles per hour.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Arctic air once again spilled over the Divide the morning of the 8th leading to a period of gusty winds, blowing snow, moderate to heavy snowfall, icy conditions and much colder temperatures.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe driving conditions were observed on U.S. Highway 2 due to a combination of falling snow and blowing snow as winds gusted to 50 mph. Power outages were also noted around Bad Rock Canyon and Swan Lake due to falling trees.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Arctic air once again spilled over the Divide the morning of the 8th leading to a period of gusty winds, blowing snow, moderate to heavy snowfall, icy conditions and much colder temperatures.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A band of heavy snow developed over Missoula and the northern Bitterroot Valley during the morning commute on the 9th. Numerous traffic accidents occurred on key travel corridors as snow turned to slush and then froze to the roadways.  A total of 5.7 inches of snow was observed at the Missoula International Airport.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall of 6 to 10 inches, snow covered roads and reduced visibilities occurred across the area.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Peak wind gust measured 5 miles ESE of Pendroy.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Arctic air once again spilled over the Divide the morning of the 8th leading to a period of gusty winds, blowing snow, moderate to heavy snowfall, icy conditions and much colder temperatures.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe driving conditions occurred in the Flathead and Mission valleys the evening of the 8th through the morning of the 9th due to snow and blowing snow.  A peak wind gust of 56 mph occurred at Glacier Park International Airport at 4:46 pm on the 8th during the evening commute with minor power outages also noted.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: After a relatively mild January and first few days of February, an arctic air mass came over the Continental Divide into Northwest Montana during the morning of February 3 bringing strong winds, heavy snow, and localized freezing rain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An arctic airmass rushed over the Continental Divide the morning of the 3rd, reaching Essex around 11 am and followed the U.S. Highway 2 corridor into West Glacier by 2 pm. Blizzard conditions were observed as falling snow combined with wind gusts of 45 to 55 miles per hour.  A peak wind gust of 57 mph was observed at Essex with gusts over 40 mph for 9 consecutive hours, resulting in an extended period of severe driving conditions.  An avalanche official reported these were the worst conditions he had seen in his 13 years in the Essex area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another push of arctic air came back over the Continental Divide the evening of the 4th, reaching Kalispell during the morning commute on the 5th. Winds were not as strong along U.S. Highway 2 as the previous day but were stronger in Kalispell.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow and blowing snow on the morning of the 5th caused poor driving conditions and numerous accidents during the morning rush hour. The peak wind gust of 39 mph at Glacier Park International Airport occurred at 8:01 am.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Arctic air pushed across the Divide and into the Kalispell area on the evening of 14th leading to a period of moderate snow and gusty winds with associated impacts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe driving conditions occurred in the Flathead Valley due to a combination of snow, blowing snow, and flash-frozen roads. A peak wind gust of 41 mph was observed at the Glacier Park International Airport, along with 4 inches of new snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow fell ahead of a warm front on the 17th, especially across Northwest Montana.  An Arctic front brought strong winds and additional snow late on the 17th into the 18th across all of western Montana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow, blowing snow, and a flash freeze led to severe driving conditions late on the 17th into the 18th.  Wind gusts of 30 to 40 mph on the morning of the 18th combined with snow to produce greatly reduced visibilities.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow fell ahead of a warm front on the 17th, especially across Northwest Montana.  An Arctic front brought strong winds and additional snow late on the 17th into the 18th across all of western Montana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe driving conditions were observed on major travel routes due to snow and blowing snow. Wind gusts of 50 to 60 mph were common creating localized ground blizzard conditions.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow fell ahead of a warm front on the 17th, especially across Northwest Montana.  An Arctic front brought strong winds and additional snow late on the 17th into the 18th across all of western Montana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous slide off crashes and jackknifed semi trucks occurred on Interstate 90 due to snow and blowing snow.  Widespread snowfall amounts of 8 to 12 inches were common with wind gusts of 35 to 45 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another push of arctic air came back over the Continental Divide the evening of the 4th, reaching Kalispell during the morning commute on the 5th. Winds were not as strong along U.S. Highway 2 as the previous day but were stronger in Kalispell.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A snow slide occurred across State Highway 1 at mile marker 27.5 near Georgetown Lake early on the morning of the 5th.  The northbound lane was impassable with up to 15 inches of snow on the southbound lane.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Clearing skies and light winds accompanying an Arctic ridge of high pressure resulted in significant nocturnal cooling on New Year's Eve evening into the morning of New Year's Day, prompting a wind chill warning for portions of North-Central Montana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: COOP station near Rudyard reported wind chill of -51F with sustained wind of 12 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy upslope snow fell across the mountains of northwest Montana on the 24th into the 25th, with snow bands leading to isolated heavy snow late on the 25th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Significant impacts were felt on major travel corridors. Interstate 90 was blocked several times due to jackknifed semi trucks in addition to periodic severe driving conditions. A 24 hour snow amount of 25 inches of snow was reported at Lookout Pass.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A Pacific cold front accompanied by a sharp surface low pressure trough crossed North-Central and Southwest Montana during the late morning through evening of March 9th. This front was followed by a period of breezy and westerly downslope winds. In several locations, these winds gusted to warning-criteria strength.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sieben Flats Montana DOT station recorded peak gust of 59 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Unsettled northwesterly flow aloft resided over North-Central Montana on March 26th into 27th. Within this flow, a shortwave trough moved southeastward over the region during late morning through early evening of the 27th. Along and ahead of this shortwave, a 50 to 60 knot 700 mb jet streak resulted in strong cross-barrier flow along the Rocky Mountain Front. This jet also extended eastward over much of North-Central Montana. The strong cross-barrier flow and associated enhanced mountain wave activity likely contributed to the high winds along the Rocky Mountain Front. In addition, several high wind gusts occurred along the plains of eastern Glacier County as well as Cascade, Chouteau, and Judith Basin Counties. In these areas, a combination of downslope flow and diurnal atmospheric mixing tapping into the 50 to 60 knot 700 mb jet streak likely contributed to the high wind gusts on the 27th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Peak gust of 61 mph recorded at WFO Great Falls.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper-level shortwave trough moved from the Pacific Northwest and northern California to central Montana and western Wyoming from the early evening of March 3rd through late evening of March 4th. At 700 mb, a low- to mid-level shortwave trough shifted from Idaho and vicinity to eastern Montana. Simultaneously, a low pressure center developed within this shortwave. Horizontal wind shear associated with the counter-clockwise, inward circulation of this low led to weak 850-700 mb frontogenesis and associated ascent over North-Central and Southwest Montana. Large-scale ascent associated with the aforementioned frontogenesis and upper-level divergence ahead of the shortwave trough, combined with sufficient moisture, led to the development of snow over the region. At the surface, a front meandered near the Rocky Mountain Front and the mountains near Great Falls. This front separated cold air to its north from relatively-milder air to its south. This system delivered significant snow amounts to portions of North-Central and Southwest Montana, while the heaviest snow was focused east of I-15. While snow fell March 3rd through 5th, the bulk of accumulation occurred on the 3rd and 4th. In addition, Great Falls set a new snowfall record for March 4th (5.1 inches). The previous record was 4.0 inches set in 1918.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: MT-66 closed in Blaine County.

This system delivered accumulating snow to most of the mountains of North-Central and Southwest Montana. Most lower elevations in both regions experienced a mix of rain and snow, with minor snow accumulations and impacts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Montana DOT reported chains required on MT-200 from Rogers Pass to junction with Flesher Pass Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An arctic front, accompanied by snow, moved across the Billings Forecast Area during the afternoon and evening of the 30th. In addition, Pacific moisture moved overhead across the arctic air mass during the evening of the 30th and into the early morning hours of the 31st. This resulted in heavy snow across most of the Billings Forecast area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall of 6 to 7 inches was reported across the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Tight east-west pressure gradients across the area resulted in high wind gusts across the western portions of the Billings Forecast Area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A wind gust of 75 mph was recorded at the Stillwater Mine.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Tight surface pressure gradients across the area resulted in a few high wind gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A wind gust of 59 mph was recorded in the Big Timber area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Tight surface pressure gradients across the area resulted in a few high wind gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A wind gust of 58 mph was recorded at the Billings Airport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Sub-freezing air temperatures combined with freezing temperatures caused untreated surfaces to become slick.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Temperatures were in the upper 20s when freezing rain occurred and created slick driving conditions across the Flathead Valley.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An atmospheric river impacting the western U.S. pushed a steady plume of moisture over a cold air mass. Temperatures ranged from near zero along the Hi-Line to the 40s in the southwest valleys. The first round of snow began late Wednesday night, April  4th, and peaked early Thursday (April 5th) with a broad band of snow across central MT. After a brief lull, wherein daylight warmed road surface temperatures enough to cause melting, the next round of snow began to develop Thursday afternoon. Snow continued Thursday night into Friday morning (April 6th) while temperature dropped further. Both systems produced snow on icy roads just in time for the morning drive Thursday and Friday mornings.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Persistent light to moderate snow caused significant travel impacts at pass level in southern Lewis and Clark County, including the closure of Rogers Pass. Severe driving conditions were reported from Rogers Pass to the junction with HWY 434.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A compact shortwave over the Pacific Northwest raced across southern Canada, delivering a period of strong winds to parts of Central Montana. The wind was due to a combination of anomalously strong H700 and H850 winds associated with the approaching shortwave, strong diurnal mixing, passage of the cold front, and decent pressure rises (behind the |front).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An atmospheric river impacting the western U.S. pushed a steady plume of moisture over a cold air mass. Temperatures ranged from near zero along the Hi-Line to the 40s in the southwest valleys. The first round of snow began late Wednesday night, April  4th, and peaked early Thursday (April 5th) with a broad band of snow across central MT. After a brief lull, wherein daylight warmed road surface temperatures enough to cause melting, the next round of snow began to develop Thursday afternoon. Snow continued Thursday night into Friday morning (April 6th) while temperature dropped further. Both systems produced snow on icy roads just in time for the morning drive Thursday and Friday mornings.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A band of heavier snow developed over Judith Basin County, producing a significant amount of snow up to 12 inches per the DOT. A spotter in the area reported 7 inches of snow and 1 to 2 foot drifts. The heavy snow was followed by breezy conditions, causing blowing and drifting of the snow. The DOT reported severe driving conditions from Utica to Hobson.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A 31-year-old Miles City woman died in Rosebud County after she was rear-ended while slowing for a crash in front of her. The fatal crash occurred east of the town of Rosebud on I-94 west of mile marker 108 at about 11:04 a.m.. The woman was driving a sedan and slowed after the passenger car in front of her went off the road, hit a guardrail on the left side of the road and then spun back across a lane of traffic in front of her in the right lane. That crash produced a large cloud of snow and other debris which greatly limited visibility behind it. After the woman's car slowed for the crash ahead, she was rear-ended by an SUV driving behind her, sending her vehicle into the guardrail on the right side of the road. The woman was not wearing a seat belt and the collision ejected her out of the car.

A strong surface low pressure system moved into the region from Alberta and Saskatchewan on January 9th, departing on the 11th. Meanwhile, a strong northwesterly upper level jet moved across the area on the 10th and 11th, enhancing lift and producing accumulating snow across northeast Montana. In addition to widespread areas of 2 to 5 inches of snowfall, strong winds were brought down to the surface, causing travel difficulties in the form of blowing and drifting snow across roadways as well as reduced visibility. Road conditions were rapidly deteriorating by the morning of the 10th, and did not improve until they were plowed on the 11th and 12th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Reports on the KLTZ radio station's social media page indicated that several people in the town of St Marie could not get out of their driveways due to blowing and drifting snow, and that even an ambulance had to call for a snow plow to get in and out of town. New snowfall amounts ranged from approximately 2 to 5 inches. Time is estimated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Behind a strong cold front that brought a few inches of snow to northeast Montana the last week of December, northwesterly flow returned, bringing very cold weather and dangerous wind chills to the area beginning on New Year's Eve and lasting through January 2nd of the new year. Wind chills dropped to less than 50 below zero in a few locations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Temperatures were in the teens below zero much of the morning of the 1st. With easterly winds at 10 to 15 mph, wind chills were down into the 30s below zero across Garfield County, and a wind chill of 40 below was recorded at the Mesonet site near Sand Springs at 7 AM.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Behind a strong cold front that brought a few inches of snow to northeast Montana the last week of December, northwesterly flow returned, bringing very cold weather and dangerous wind chills to the area beginning on New Year's Eve and lasting through January 2nd of the new year. Wind chills dropped to less than 50 below zero in a few locations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: With temperatures in the teens below zero and winds gusting above 30 mph at times, wind chills across portions of Sheridan County were less than 40 below zero for several hours on the evening of the 1st and into the early morning hours of the 2nd.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Behind a strong cold front that brought a few inches of snow to northeast Montana the last week of December, northwesterly flow returned, bringing very cold weather and dangerous wind chills to the area beginning on New Year's Eve and lasting through January 2nd of the new year. Wind chills dropped to less than 50 below zero in a few locations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: With bitter cold temperatures dropping into the 30s below zero and easterly winds of approximately 10 mph, wind chills dropped into the 40s and even 50s below zero across western Roosevelt County, with the lowest wind chill recorded being 59 below zero at the Poplar RAWS at 8:18 AM MST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Behind a strong cold front that brought a few inches of snow to northeast Montana the last week of December, northwesterly flow returned, bringing very cold weather and dangerous wind chills to the area beginning on New Year's Eve and lasting through January 2nd of the new year. Wind chills dropped to less than 50 below zero in a few locations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Bitter cold temperatures and relatively light easterly winds produced wind chills of less than 50 below zero early on the morning of the 1st across portions of Valley County. The coldest wind chill recorded in the county was at the Glasgow ASOS, which recorded a wind chill of 54 degrees below zero at 3:53 AM on January 1st. This event was a continuation from the evening of December 31st, when the Glasgow ASOS recorded a wind chill of 56 degrees below zero.

Wind chills dropped to less than 50 below zero in a few locations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: With bitter cold temperatures and winds gusting to approximately 20 mph, wind chills were able to drop to less than 40 degrees below zero at a couple of locations across McCone County on the evening of the 1st. The lowest recorded wind chill was 46 degrees below zero at the Cow Creek DOT site.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Behind a strong cold front that brought a few inches of snow to northeast Montana the last week of December, northwesterly flow returned, bringing very cold weather and dangerous wind chills to the area beginning on New Year's Eve and lasting through January 2nd of the new year. Wind chills dropped to less than 50 below zero in a few locations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Bitter cold temperatures and winds gusting to approximately 25 mph contributed to the production of extreme wind chill values of less than 40 degrees below zero over portions of Dawson County from the evening of the 1st into the early morning hours of the 2nd. The coldest wind chill recorded was 46 degrees below zero at 8:43 PM MST on the 1st.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Behind a strong cold front that brought a few inches of snow to northeast Montana the last week of December, northwesterly flow returned, bringing very cold weather and dangerous wind chills to the area beginning on New Year's Eve and lasting through January 2nd of the new year. EVENT_NARRATIVE: Bitter cold temperatures and winds gusting to approximately 25 mph contributed to the production of wind chill values of less than 30 degrees below zero over portions of Prairie County on the 1st, and once dropped values to less than 40 below. The coldest wind chill recorded was 48 degrees below zero at 4:08 AM MST on the 1st, recorded at the Big Sheep Mountain RAWS.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold, arctic air mass pushed the jet stream southward over the region and directed strong and gusty west winds across portions of central and northeast Montana. Some of the strongest winds from aloft were able to reach the surface, especially in southwest Phillips County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Winds at the Montana DOT site 35 miles south of Malta gusted to 58 miles per hour or greater between 7:11 AM MST and 7:30 AM MST on the morning of the 30th. The peak gust was 61 miles per hour at 7:11 AM MST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Very little drought relief was observed during the month of January despite very cold temperatures. While most of northeast Montana saw at least some snow, snowfall amounts were not enough in most areas to make much of an impact on the drought situation.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The month of January began with central and southeast Phillips county experiencing severe (D2) drought conditions. Even though a couple of locations received 4 to 6 inches of snow during the month, there was no significant recovery in the drought by the beginning of February.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An isolated thunderstorm produced some hail and a very strong wind gust near Joliet.
An isolated thunderstorm produced some hail and a very strong wind gust near Joliet.
A thunderstorm over Carter County produced sub-severe hail.
An isolated thunderstorm produced some severe hail near Roundup and Acton.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The hail was reported along Highway 3 north of Billings.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An isolated thunderstorm produced some nickel to quarter size hail just southeast of Columbus.
An isolated thunderstorm produced some nickel to quarter size hail just southeast of Columbus.

Very cold air moved into northeast Montana behind a cold front on the morning of the 12th. Wind chill values dropped into the 20s and 30s below zero across much of the region. Extreme wind chills of less than 40 below zero were recorded across western Roosevelt County, lasting from the morning of the 12th into the morning of the 13th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind chills at both the Poplar RAWS and the Comertown Montana Department of Transportation sites dropped to less than 40 below zero from approximately 8:15 AM MST on the 12th, remaining below or near 40 below zero until approximately 7:15 AM MST on the 13th. Winds were generally from the south, then shifted to the northeast at approximately 10 miles per hour.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An overrunning system brought snow and a brief arctic intrusion along the Continental Divide. An upper level warm front brought heavy snowfall during the evening of the 11th from central Idaho to areas along and east of the Idaho and Montana border and created difficult travel.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A period of moderate to heavy snow moved over the region by the evening of the 11th creating low visibility and slick roads. Total snowfall amounts ranged between 6 and 7 inches from Trout Creek to Bull Lake.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An overrunning system brought snow and a brief arctic intrusion along the Continental Divide. An upper level warm front brought heavy snowfall during the evening of the 11th from central Idaho to areas along and east of the Idaho and Montana border and created difficult travel.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An arctic cold front moved west along US-2 past Marias Pass and Essex by noon on the 10th causing wind gusts between 40 and 50 mph and periods of moderate to heavy snow, low visibility less than 500 feet at times, blowing and drifting snow. Snowfall amounts from this first part of the storm included 2 inches at Essex, 4.1 at West Glacier to 7 inches towards Marias Pass. Another 2 to 3 inches of snow fell between the 11th and 12th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An overrunning system brought snow and a brief arctic intrusion along the Continental Divide. An upper level warm front brought heavy snowfall during the evening of the 11th from central Idaho to areas along and east of the Idaho and Montana border and created difficult travel.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Moderate snow fell across the region while blowing and drifting snow occurred in the Polson area due to gusty north winds off of Flathead Lake. Total Snowfall amounts included 4 to 6 inches in the Flathead Valley and closer to 7 inches in Polson. The Polson schools had a 2 hour delay on the 12th. A 82 year old woman died as a result of injuries sustained in a two-vehicle accident on Montana Highway 40 between Columbia Falls and Whitefish. Slippery road conditions were one of the suspected factors in the crash.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An overrunning system brought snow and a brief arctic intrusion along the Continental Divide. An upper level warm front brought heavy snowfall during the evening of the 11th from central Idaho to areas along and east of the Idaho and Montana border and created difficult travel.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The COOP observers at Lindbergh lake and Seeley Lake reported a total snowfall total of 19.1 and 17.0 inches respectively during this storm. Because of the heavy snowfall rates during the late evening hours of the 11th, tree damage and intermittent power outages were reported in these areas.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong winter storm system moved across the area, bringing significant snowfall to the region from the morning of the 4th through the evening of the 5th. Snowfall amounts for most locations were generally 6 to 12 inches, with some areas in the southwest along the Musselshell River only receiving 2 to 4 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several inches of snow fell across central and southern Phillips County with this storm. Widespread snowfall amounts were 4 to 7 inches, with up to 12 inches reported at the Montana Department of Transportation site south of Malta on Highway 191. Montana road reports showed snow and blowing snow significantly impacting travel conditions as early as noon on the 4th. Highway 191 was closed due to the severe travel conditions from approximately 8:00 on the evening of March 4th and did not reopen until the early morning hours of March 6th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong winter storm system moved across the area, bringing significant snowfall to the region from the morning of the 4th through the evening of the 5th. Snowfall amounts for most locations were generally 6 to 12 inches, with some areas in the southwest along the Musselshell River only receiving 2 to 4 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Montana road reports show blowing and drifting snow significantly impacting travel as early as noon on the 4th of March. By 8 AM on the 5th, there were multiple reports from the Zortman area of 12 to 15 inches of new snowfall with drifts over 4 feet deep.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong winter storm system moved across the area, bringing significant snowfall to the region from the morning of the 4th through the evening of the 5th. Snowfall amounts for most locations were generally 6 to 12 inches, with some areas in the southwest along the Musselshell River only receiving 2 to 4 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several inches of snow fell across northern Valley County with this storm. Snowfall amounts of up to 14 inches were reported with this storm, with 10 inches falling on the 4th alone at a cooperative observation site north of Opheim.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On March 30th, a 700 mb jet strengthened over Montana, while daytime warming yielded a deep, well-mixed boundary layer during the late morning and afternoon hours. In addition, a Canadian cold front began to push southward into North-Central Montana during the afternoon. Vertical mixing of the boundary layer ahead of and behind the front allowed the strong winds aloft to be transported down to the surface. Accordingly, several locations reported warning-criteria peak wind gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mesonet station recorded 61 mph peak wind gust 14-miles NNW of Joplin.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong winter storm system moved across the area, bringing significant snowfall to the region from the morning of the 4th through the evening of the 5th. Snowfall amounts for most locations were generally 6 to 12 inches, with some areas in the southwest along the Musselshell River only receiving 2 to 4 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Very sparse snowfall reports came in from Daniels County for this storm. However, Montana DOT road reports showed severe driving conditions across Daniels County due to blowing and drifting snow beginning at 8:30 PM on the 4th, and lasting into the evening of the 5th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong winter storm system moved across the area, bringing significant snowfall to the region from the morning of the 4th through the evening of the 5th. Snowfall amounts for most locations were generally 6 to 12 inches, with some areas in the southwest along the Musselshell River only receiving 2 to 4 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow began falling in Sheridan County at around 7 AM on March 4th. Snowfall diminished overnight on the 4th, but returned with gusty winds the morning of the 5th. By 6:30 AM, blowing and drifting snow had reduced visibility to blizzard conditions, and these conditions persisted until around 6 PM on the 5th. Snow stopped falling at around 11 PM on the 5th. Four to six inches of snowfall was reported across Sheridan County from this storm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong winter storm system moved across the area, bringing significant snowfall to the region from the morning of the 4th through the evening of the 5th. Snowfall amounts for most locations were generally 6 to 12 inches, with some areas in the southwest along the Musselshell River only receiving 2 to 4 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Eight to twelve inches of snow were reported across Petroleum County, most of which fell on March 4th. Eight inches of snow was reported by a cooperative observer 6 miles NNE  of Winnett at 10PM on March 4th, and a trained spotter reported a storm total of 12 inches of snow in Winnett at 9 AM on March 5th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong winter storm system moved across the area, bringing significant snowfall to the region from the morning of the 4th through the evening of the 5th. Snowfall amounts for most locations were generally 6 to 12 inches, with some areas in the southwest along the Musselshell River only receiving 2 to 4 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow fell across portions of Garfield County the evening of March 4th through much of March 5th. At 8:30 AM MST on the 5th, a trained spotter located 7 miles southwest of the Hell Creek Recreation Area reported an estimated 12 inches of snow with drifts six to seven feet deep and reduced visibility due to wind gusts of 35 to 45 mph. Additionally, at 5 PM MST on the 5th a cooperative observer 3 miles north of Brusett reported a 24 hour snowfall total of 12 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong winter storm system moved across the area, bringing significant snowfall to the region from the morning of the 4th through the evening of the 5th. Snowfall amounts for most locations were generally 6 to 12 inches, with some areas in the southwest along the Musselshell River only receiving 2 to 4 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall reports were very sparse across McCone County, but reports show that over 4 inches of snow fell across the area on March 4th and 5th. Blowing and drifting snow caused significantly reduced visibility and severe travel conditions, especially after 9:00 PM MST March 4th through most of the day on the 5th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: This longer-duration winter event was really made up of 3 separate systems. The first wave brought snow and mixed precipitation to Central Montana on Friday, February 2. After a lull, another round of mostly snow impacted Central Montana on Saturday, February 3. The third, and final, wave brought snow and mixed precipitation to Central and Southwest Montana Sunday-Monday, February 4-5.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm spotter measured a 6 inch snow depth with blowing and drifting snow. Visibility under a quarter mile with sideroads barely passable.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Late in the afternoon of February 14th, a cold front began moving south across North-Central Montana. Along and just ahead of the front, a band of moderate to heavy snow developed. That band of snow eventually shifted south, impacting much of Southwest Montana, although snowfall rates lessened somewhat there. Behind the front, strong cold air advection combined with strong winds aloft produced widespread wind gusts of 30 to 50 mph. Also behind the front, areas of moderate to heavy upslope-enhanced snow developed along and north of the Little Belt Mountains. The combination of wind and snow allowed many areas in North-Central Montana to experience blizzard-like conditions for about one to two hours before the wind and snow began to subside late on the 14th and into the morning of the 15th. Severe driving conditions and several road closures were reported across much of the region. At the request of local sheriff���s offices, emergency travel only was requested for every county in North-Central Montana and three counties in Southwest Montana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Spotter located 8 miles S of Belt reported heavy snow and blowing snow were causing blizzard-like conditions. Estimated 6-inches of new snow since 16:00 MST, Feb 14th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong winter storm system moved across the area, bringing significant snowfall to the region from the morning of the 4th through the evening of the 5th. However, gusty winds, a much above normal snowpack, and fresh snow contributed to create severe driving conditions due to reduced visibility from blowing and drifting of snow across roadways. These conditions prevailed from the afternoon of March 4th through the morning of the 5th.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall reports were difficult to come by, with 4 to 6 inches of snow being reported across Dawson County. However, the snow and gusty winds created hazardous travel conditions due to snow blowing and drifting across roadways. Blizzard conditions were observed at Glendive through much of the early morning hours of March 5th. This was concurrent with winds gusting to 50 mph or greater. The hazardous travel conditions across Dawson County persisted from the early morning hours of March 4th through the morning hours of the 5th.

Additionally, gusty winds contributed to blowing and drifting snow across roadways throughout this period.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Blizzard conditions with visibility of a quarter mile or less due to blowing snow were observed by the Sidney airport AWOS. Wind gusts were 35 to 45 mph during this time. Additionally, by late morning the Richland County's Sheriff's office was advising emergency travel only due to blizzard conditions impacting travel.

This caused significantly reduced visibility, and visibility was below or near a quarter mile until shortly after 5 PM on the 5th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the 7th through 9th, North-Central and Southwest Montana resided underneath northwesterly flow aloft associated with the polar front jet stream. In addition, multiple disturbances embedded within this flow interacted with a frontal boundary that wavered over the area from southwest to northeast and vice versa. The resulting lift, combined with abundant moisture in the low- to mid-level atmosphere, contributed to the formation of periods of snow over North-Central and Southwest Montana. In addition, it is believed mesoscale bands of heavy snow that affected portions of North-Central Montana on the 8th into early morning of the 9th were tied to frontogenesis in the 850 mb to 700 mb layer.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Estimated snowfall rates of 1.0 to 1.5-inches per hour at Great Falls WFO as heavy snow bands shifted southward over the Great Falls area. Visibility was reduced to near 1/4 mile.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The drought extending from the previous year persisted through March across portions of northeast Montana. There were areas of improvement, specifically across Valley and Garfield counties and areas west, but otherwise drought conditions remained the same throughout the month of March.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Northern Valley County entered March experiencing Severe (D2) drought conditions. By March 20th, conditions had improved to Moderate (D1) drought conditions. This was due to the two to three inches of liquid equivalent precipitation that fell during the month, which was approximately twice the normal amount for the area. This occurred primarily during the first week of the month, during which a foot or more of snow fell.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The spring thaw through the central Montana region allowed ice to break up along the Musselshell River.  The meandering nature of the river and the sudden warm up in temperatures resulted in a brief period of ice jam flooding near the Mosby area of southeastern Petroleum County and southwestern Garfield County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: During the evening of March 12st, at the Mosby river forecast point (MSBM8) along the Musselshell River, an ice jam suddenly caused the river level to briefly rise above the flood stage of 10 feet at 5:24 PM MST. Even though the river fell back down below 10 feet just 9 minutes later, at 5:33 PM MST, it quickly rose above 10 feet again at 6:14 PM MST. The river crested at 11.53 feet at 6:59 PM MST. As the ice jam cleared, the river fell back down below flood stage at 7:20 PM MST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A series of 700 mb weather disturbances moved generally eastward over North-Central Montana between late evening of February 22nd and early evening of the 24th. During the same period, 700 mb winds became more westerly and strengthened, overall, along and east of the Rocky Mountain Front. This caused a surface trough of low pressure east of the Rocky Mountain Front to deepen, especially during late morning of the 23rd through early morning of the 24th. As the trough deepened, a tightening pressure gradient drove strong, gusty, and westerly surface winds, leading to blizzard conditions along the Rocky Mountain Front.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Blizzard conditions reported 17 miles WSW of Bynum by trained spotter. Near-zero visibility in significant blowing snow. Snow drifts up to about 20 feet.

During the same period, 700 mb winds became more westerly and strengthened, overall, along and east of the Rocky Mountain Front. This caused a surface trough of low pressure east of the Rocky Mountain Front to deepen, especially during late morning of the 23rd through early morning of the 24th. As the trough deepened, a tightening pressure gradient drove strong, gusty, and westerly surface winds, leading to blizzard conditions along the Rocky Mountain Front.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Peak wind gust of 82 mph reported by trained spotter in Babb. Spotter also noted significant blowing snow reducing visibility to near a quarter mile.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Northwesterly flow aloft persisted over North-Central Montana during the late morning of February 15th through morning of the 16th. Within this flow, a disturbance at 700 mb traversed North-Central Montana during late evening of the 15th and early morning of the 16th. This disturbance caused the 700 mb flow to become more westerly and strengthen across the Rocky Mountain Front. In response, a surface trough east of the Rocky Mountain Front strengthened. This caused the surface pressure gradient to tighten, which in turn allowed westerly winds to intensify along the Rocky Mountain Front. These strong winds, combined with a loose snow pack, led to a blizzard along the Northern Rocky Mountain Front.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Montana DOT reported severe driving conditions on US-2 from East Glacier to Browning and along US-89 from Two Medicine Creek to Browning. These severe driving conditions stemmed from significant blowing and drifting snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Late in the afternoon of February 14th, a cold front began moving south across North-Central Montana. Along and just ahead of the front, a band of moderate to heavy snow developed. That band of snow eventually shifted south, impacting much of Southwest Montana, although snowfall rates lessened somewhat there. Behind the front, strong cold air advection combined with strong winds aloft produced widespread wind gusts of 30 to 50 mph. Also behind the front, areas of moderate to heavy upslope-enhanced snow developed along and north of the Little Belt Mountains. The combination of wind and snow allowed many areas in North-Central Montana to experience blizzard-like conditions for about one to two hours before the wind and snow began to subside late on the 14th and into the morning of the 15th. Severe driving conditions and several road closures were reported across much of the region. At the request of local sheriff���s offices, emergency travel only was requested for every county in North-Central Montana and three counties in Southwest Montana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow chains required on towing units along US-12 from MacDonald Pass to Helena.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy, wet snow ranging from 3 to 10 inches fell during the morning hours with rates of up to 2 inches per hour. Travel was significantly impacted with minor power outages and a few tree branches pulled down.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Freezing drizzle, fog, gusty 40 mph winds, along with snow and blowing snow were reported across the area. This all resulted in very poor visibilities. State highways were closed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The drought extending from the previous year persisted through March across portions of northeast Montana. There were areas of improvement, specifically across Valley and Garfield counties and areas west, but otherwise drought conditions remained the same throughout the month of March.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Western Roosevelt County entered March experiencing Severe (D2) drought conditions, which persisted through the month, although improvement was seen by April 3rd. The area received generally one to two inches of liquid equivalent precipitation, which was up to twice the normal amount.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A surface low centered over far southern Montana and northern Wyoming was able to pull a cold front across northeast Montana on the evening of the 30th. This, in addition to the presence of northwest flow aloft and mid-level moisture advection, produced snow showers across the area. Although only a few inches of snow fell during this time, gusty winds developed in response to a strong pressure gradient associated with the surface low. As a result, blowing and drifting snow began to impact driving conditions along the Canadian border where winds were stronger.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Montana Department of Highways archived road reports show that significant blowing and drifting snow created hazardous driving conditions across Sheridan County during the afternoon and early evening hours of March 30th.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow amounts up to 10 inches were reported near the Dakota border.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Central and southern Valley County entered March experiencing Severe (D2) drought conditions. By March 20th, conditions had improved to Moderate (D1) drought conditions. This was due to the two to three inches of liquid equivalent precipitation that fell during the month, which was approximately twice the normal amount for the area. This occurred primarily during the first week of the month, during which a foot or more of snow fell.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Seasonal spring-time temperatures consistently above freezing allowed the Milk River and its smaller tributaries to begin flowing.  This raised the level of the Milk River along most of its official forecast points through the latter half of April.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Excessive snow melt caused flooding at the stream gauge at Beaver Creek near Hinsdale (BCHM8). The gauge recorded stream levels reaching flood stage at 2:45 AM MST on April 16th. The river crested at 17.42 feet at 3:30 AM MST on April 18th, then fell steadily, dropping below flood stage at 6:00 PM MST on April 20th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Seasonal spring-time temperatures consistently above freezing allowed the Milk River and its smaller tributaries to begin flowing.  This raised the level of the Milk River along most of its official forecast points through the latter half of April.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Excessive snow melt caused flooding at the stream gauge at Beaver Creek near Saco (GSCM8). The gauge recorded stream levels reaching flood stage at 1:45 AM MST on April 17th. The river crested at 11.3 feet at 9:30 PM MST on April 20th, then fell steadily, dropping below flood stage at 1:45 PM MST on April 22th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The month of April began with Severe (D2) drought conditions still being experienced by Sheridan and eastern Daniels counties. By the end of the month, enough precipitation had fallen over the area to relieve this somewhat, with no areas remaining in the Severe drought category by the end of April. Sheridan, Daniels, and Roosevelt counties were still experiencing Moderate (D1) drought conditions, however.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe (D2) drought conditions were experienced across eastern Daniels County throughout the month of April. The drought was relieved by one to three inches of liquid water equivalent in the area, which was more than twice the normal amount of precipitation for April. Conditions were shown to have improved by the Drought Monitor on May 1st.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Warming temperatures led to river flooding along the lower Milk River beginning during the latter part of April. Flooding had already been occurring on the Milk River and some of its tributaries upstream since April 16th, and flooding on the lower Milk River began on the 25th. Flooding continued into the early part of May.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Excessive snow melt caused flooding at the river gauge on the Milk River near Nashua (NSHM8). The gauge recorded river levels reaching flood stage at 8:30 PM MST on April 25th. The river crested at 22.35 feet at 2:15 PM MST on April 30th. River levels remained at or above flood stage into May.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Ice jamming created flooding along some of the tributaries of the Milk River early in the month, especially along portions of Beaver Creek. Flooding receded once the ice had begun to break up and progressed downstream.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The river gage along Beaver Creek near Saco (GSCM8) first entered flood stage at 8:15 PM MST on April 3rd due to ice jamming along the creek. The gage recorded periods of flooding before the ice began to break up and continue downstream. The river crested at 12.35 feet at 8:30 PM MST on April 6th. The river fell below flood stage for the final time at 4:00 AM MST on April 7th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong late-season winter storm moved across the northern Rockies from the Pacific Northwest on the evening of the 1st, bringing gusty winds and accumulating snow to areas mainly south of the Missouri River through the 2nd. The heaviest snowfall occurred across Garfield and Petroleum counties, where four to six inches of snow was reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: This storm began with rain on the evening of April 1st. As temperatures dropped overnight, the precipitation transitioned to snow, and the Montana Department of Transportation observed roads were snow covered and icy by the morning of the 2nd. Additionally, winds gusting to 25 to 30 mph were causing travel difficulties across the Little Rockies and southwestern Phillips County. These conditions persisted into the evening of April 2nd. Snowfall totals from this storm ranged from 2 to 4 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong late-season winter storm moved across the northern Rockies from the Pacific Northwest on the evening of the 1st, bringing gusty winds and accumulating snow to areas mainly south of the Missouri River through the 2nd. The heaviest snowfall occurred across Garfield and Petroleum counties, where four to six inches of snow was reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: This storm began with rain on the evening of April 1st. As temperatures dropped overnight, the precipitation transitioned to snow, and the Montana Department of Transportation observed roads were snow covered and icy. Additionally, winds gusting to 25 to 30 mph were causing travel difficulties across Petroleum County. These conditions persisted into the evening of April 2nd. Snowfall totals from cooperative observers in this storm ranged from 4 inches at Winnett 12 SW to 8 inches at Flatwillow 4 ENE.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A springtime snow storm cut across the Rocky Mountains and southern Montana on April 12th and 13th. This storm brought nearly a foot of snow to Petroleum County and up to 6 inches across portions of Garfield, McCone, Dawson, and Wibaux counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Along with a snowfall report of 5 new inches from a cooperative observer at Wibaux 2 E, the Montana Department of Transportation road reports showed treacherous travel conditions along area highways due to snowy and icy conditions from approximately 11 PM MDT, which persisted through the early morning hours.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Along with a snowfall report of 2 new inches from a cooperative observer in Terry, Montana Department of Transportation road reports showed treacherous travel conditions along area highways due to snowy and icy conditions from approximately 11 PM MDT, which persisted through the early morning hours.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The mesonet site at the airport in Poplar recorded nearly 7 hours of continuous dense freezing fog with visibility at a quarter of a mile or less.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Seasonal spring-time temperatures consistently above freezing allowed the Milk River and its smaller tributaries to begin flowing.  This raised the level of the Milk River along most of its official forecast points through the latter half of April.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Excessive snow melt caused flooding at the stream gauge at Beaver Creek near Saco (GSCM8). The gauge recorded stream levels reaching flood stage at 1:45 AM MST on April 17th. The river crested at 11.3 feet at 9:30 PM MST on April 20th, then fell steadily, dropping below flood stage at 1:45 PM MST on April 22th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Enough instability existed due to an upper level trough and jet streak to produce multiple thunderstorms along the Yellowstone River valley of eastern Montana during the afternoon and evening. A few of these thunderstorms became severe near Terry and Glendive, with the strongest wind gusts exceeding 60 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 60 mph peak wind gust was measured at the Glendive Airport AWOS.

This was due to thunderstorms over the area that produced over an inch and a half of rain in less than an hour. The Mesonet site TWSM8 1 mile southeast of Terry, recorded 1.68 inches of rain from these thunderstorms.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The month of April began with Severe (D2) drought conditions still being experienced by Sheridan and eastern Daniels counties. By the end of the month, enough precipitation had fallen over the area to relieve this somewhat, with no areas remaining in the Severe drought category by the end of April. Sheridan, Daniels, and Roosevelt counties were still experiencing Moderate (D1) drought conditions, however.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe (D2) drought conditions were experienced across much of Sheridan County throughout the month of April. The drought was relieved by half an inch to one and a half inches of liquid water equivalent in the area, which was up to 150 percent of the normal amount of precipitation for April. Conditions were shown to have improved by the Drought Monitor to Moderate (D1) drought conditions April 17th for the eastern two thirds of the county, and on May 1st for the western third of the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Mid-season snowmelt runoff from Montana's central mountains, raised the level of the Musselshell River at Mosby above flood stage for a few days.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Musselshell River at Mosby (MSBM8) rose above the flood stage of 10 feet at 12:05 pm MST on June 1st, crested at 11.1 feet at 3:55 pm MST that same day, then fell back down below 10 feet at 7:07 am MST on June 2nd.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The combination of above normal mountain snow-pack up to 200% of normal and warmer than normal temperatures in the month of May led to continued and new river flooding. Missoula County reported that the tally for damage was to the amount of $450,000.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Yaak River reached minor flood stage and affected low lying areas adjacent to the river.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Hot temperatures and sunny skies increased the temperature inside of vehicles and contributed to the heatstroke death of a child in Glendive.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Billings Gazette newspaper reported that a 2 year old male child suffered death by heatstroke hyperthermia after being left in a vehicle through the middle of the day in Glendive on June 26th. The high temperature was 83 degrees, but likely reached 125 degrees inside the enclosed vehicle. Alcohol played a part in this negligent homicide. The boy's body temperature reached 107 degrees and he was removed from life support on June 28th. Link to newspaper article: https://billingsgazette.com/news/crime/glendive-woman-accused-of-negligent-homicide-in-son-s-hot/article_c05d03ea-e68c-50ff-90b0-caea4eba8933.html|.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Isolated showers and thunderstorms developed via daytime heating of the higher terrain in central and southwest Montana during the afternoon and early evening of July 9th. These storms formed amidst monsoonal moisture, appreciable instability, and moderate to strong vertical wind shear. A cluster of storms moving northeastward from the Little Belt Mountains produced quarter-size hail on the plains of southern Judith Basin County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Spotter 9-miles southwest of Utica reported quarter-size hail and strong winds.

Several thunderstorms became severe over portions of north-central and southwest Montana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public reported quarter-size hail 3-miles SSW of Turner. Semi and trailer also reported to have been blown over in the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Southwest flow ahead of an upper level trough produced favorable conditions for severe thunderstorms generally across southwest Montana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several lodgepole pine trees were blown over and hail up to one inch were reported with this storm.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Approximately fifteen hours after the event, NWS employees found hail near the size of quarters on the side of a building in Hall. There was also evidence of very heavy rain with this storm.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were stripped of leaves and small branches.

A severe thunderstorm occurred across Carter County during the late afternoon hours of the 17th. Wind gusts to 60 mph were reported along with some small hail. However, the main impact from this thunderstorm was very heavy rain which resulted in flash flooding along Thompson Creek.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An outbuilding was blown over and there was quite a bit of tree and garden damage from the wind.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm occurred across Carter County during the late afternoon hours of the 17th. Wind gusts to 60 mph were reported along with some small hail. However, the main impact from this thunderstorm was very heavy rain which resulted in flash flooding along Thompson Creek.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Private reservoirs and stock ponds filled and overflowed. There was widespread flooding on Thompson Creek. Rainfall was estimated to be 4 to 5 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A powerful shortwave trough combined with a moderately unstable air mass, producing showers and thunderstorms across the region. A few of these became severe in the evening, especially as the trough interacted with areas of higher dewpoints close to the North Dakota and Saskatchewan borders. The most significant storms were the supercells which impacted Sheridan County, bringing significant damage to the Plentywood area in the form of a macroburst and an EF-1 tornado.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotters reported hail up to the size of quarters.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large amount of runoff from the melting of a significant snowpack contributed to prolonged flooding along the Milk River and its tributaries during mid to late April 2018.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: NWS Great Falls hydrologist reported and photographed flooding along Thirty Mile Creek just north of downtown Harlem.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unstable northwest flow aloft resulted in the development of a few severe thunderstorms across portions of the Billings Forecast Area. Large hail along with strong winds brought numerous reports of damage to cars on the west end of Billings. The storm dropped 1.11 inches of rain in 30 minutes at the National Weather Service Office on the west side of town, with the airport only receiving 0.33 inches. The hail ranged in size from pea to golf ball with the golf ball size falling across a narrow swath on the west end of town. The copious amounts of hail, mainly small, combined with the heavy rain, clogged storm drains across portions of the city. This resulted in numerous intersections and parking lots flooding with at least a foot of water.

A compact shortwave over the Pacific Northwest raced across southern Canada, delivering a period of strong winds to parts of Central Montana. The wind was due to a combination of anomalously strong H700 and H850 winds associated with the approaching shortwave, strong diurnal mixing, passage of the cold front, and decent pressure rises (behind the |front).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Initial strong wind gust measured by the Helena Portable RAWS #1.

A Pacific cold front accompanied by a sharp surface low pressure trough crossed North-Central and Southwest Montana during the late morning through evening of March 9th. This front was followed by a period of breezy and westerly downslope winds. In several locations, these winds gusted to warning-criteria strength.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public reported 60 mph peak gust 3-miles SSE of Bozeman.

A shortwave trough moving across northeast Montana ahead of a deepening upper level trough late in the evening of July 2nd, brought some showers and thunderstorms to the region. A few of these storms became strong to severe over Prairie and Wibaux Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public reported quarter size hail and wind gusts up to 40 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure system combined with favorable winds and an unstable atmosphere to support multiple severe thunderstorms across portions of northeast Montana. With relatively dry conditions at the surface in the west, damaging winds were the main result; but with more surface moisture in the east, large hail was more prevalent.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A National Weather Service employee reported multiple boat docks damaged and inaccessible at the Duck Creek Recreation Area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On March 30th, westerly upslope flow triggered snow showers, some heavy, along the Continental Divide. In addition, a Canadian cold front moving southward through North-Central and Southwest Montana��triggered showers, most of which were in the form of snow. Instability and lift along the front allowed some of these showers to organize into bands of heavy snow. These areas of snow then diminished gradually from north to south late on the 30th and into the 31st.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Montana DOT reported severe driving conditions along US-2 and US-89 in the Browning area due to accumulating snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Yet another spring storm system brought widespread precipitation (a mix of rain and snow) to a large portion of Central and Southwest Montana. It was a heavy, wet snow with many areas reporting 1���/hr snowfall rates at times. The heavier snowfall rates overcame the warmer ground and air temps, allowing snow to accumulate. This event is also noteworthy because of the significant liquid-equivalent precipitation amounts that occurred.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A band of heavy snow with snowfall rates reaching or exceeding 1 inch per hour sat over Pondera County for several hours, producing up to 10 inches of snow in a relatively short amount of time. This led to significant reductions in visibility, near whiteout conditions, and difficult travel.

CAPE values were in the range of 500-1000 J/kg along with steep lapse rates around 8.5+ C/km with freezing levels near 3km AGL.
A severe thunderstorm developed west of Hall and brought severe hail to Hall and Deerlodge.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Half dollar sized hail was reported by the public at the Deer Lodge Golf Club a few miles west of Deer Lodge.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm developed west of Hall and brought severe hail to Hall and Deerlodge.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public reported hail covering the ground southwest of Hall. Golf ball sized hail was the largest stone observed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Arctic air spilled over the Continental Divide leading to heavy snow and blowing snow which made travel difficult in parts of Northwest Montana the evening of the 1st into the morning of the 2nd.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow fell across far northwest Montana with general amounts of 6-10 inches for valleys locations.  The heaviest amounts were observed on Highway 37 south of Eureka, where 18 inches fell over 12 hours.  Travel was difficult to impossible based on public and highway department reports.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On March 30th, westerly upslope flow triggered snow showers, some heavy, along the Continental Divide. In addition, a Canadian cold front moving southward through North-Central and Southwest Montana��triggered showers, most of which were in the form of snow. Instability and lift along the front allowed some of these showers to organize into bands of heavy snow. These areas of snow then diminished gradually from north to south late on the 30th and into the 31st.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Montana DOT reported a vehicle slid-off I-15 southbound near mile marker 279.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper-level shortwave trough moved from the Pacific Northwest and northern California to central Montana and western Wyoming from the early evening of March 3rd through late evening of March 4th. At 700 mb, a low- to mid-level shortwave trough shifted from Idaho and vicinity to eastern Montana. Simultaneously, a low pressure center developed within this shortwave. Horizontal wind shear associated with the counter-clockwise, inward circulation of this low led to weak 850-700 mb frontogenesis and associated ascent over North-Central and Southwest Montana. Large-scale ascent associated with the aforementioned frontogenesis and upper-level divergence ahead of the shortwave trough, combined with sufficient moisture, led to the development of snow over the region. At the surface, a front meandered near the Rocky Mountain Front and the mountains near Great Falls. This front separated cold air to its north from relatively-milder air to its south. This system delivered significant snow amounts to portions of North-Central and Southwest Montana, while the heaviest snow was focused east of I-15. While snow fell March 3rd through 5th, the bulk of accumulation occurred on the 3rd and 4th. In addition, Great Falls set a new snowfall record for March 4th (5.1 inches). The previous record was 4.0 inches set in 1918.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: US-191 closed from Eddie's Corner to junction with US-12 near Harlowton.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The combination of above normal mountain snow-pack up to 200% of normal and warmer than normal temperatures in the month of May led to continued and new river flooding. Missoula County reported that the tally for damage was to the amount of $450,000.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Homes and buildings adjacent to Seeley Lake and the Clearwater River were impacted by flooding. Thirteen homes in the Seeley Lake region were under evacuation orders.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The combination of above normal mountain snow-pack up to 200% of normal and warmer than normal temperatures in the month of May led to continued and new river flooding. Missoula County reported that the tally for damage was to the amount of $450,000.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Low lying areas adjacent to the Clark Fork River below Missoula to Frenchtown were flooded. The river also flooded numerous homes in the vicinity of Harpers Road from both surface water and groundwater; some homes were evacuated by Missoula County Sheriff's Department. Sandbagging of homes occurred on Mallard Way to keep from flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several thunderstorms developed via daytime heating of the Rockies, near Glacier National Park, during the late afternoon of July 25th. As these storms tracked southeastward, they congealed into a cluster as their cold pools merged and interacted with ambient low-level vertical shear, resulting in the repeated formation of new storms along the downshear flank of the cold pool amidst moderate to strong effective bulk shear and weak to moderate instability. At one point, this thunderstorm cluster was a well-developed bow echo. These storms produced damaging wind gusts and hail up to quarter-size over portions of north-central MT during the evening of the 25th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: COOP observer 2-miles SSE of Bynum estimated peak gust of 80 mph and quarter-size hail with the passage of a thunderstorm. Significant damage to crops occurred.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Significant runoff from periods of rain and melting of a deep mountain snowpack allowed flooding to begin in the Lincoln area on April 29th and persist into May. Floodwaters finally receded on May 27th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lincoln Volunteer Fire Chief relayed multiple reports of flooding in and around Lincoln on April 29th and 30th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Arctic air spilled over the Continental Divide leading to heavy snow and blowing snow which made travel difficult in parts of Northwest Montana the evening of the 1st into the morning of the 2nd.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Difficult to severe driving conditions were reported in the Kalispell vicinity as well as in Lake County. Numerous accidents occurred due to a combination of ice and reduced visibility in blowing snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Arctic air spilled over the Continental Divide once again leading to strong winds and snow which made travel difficult in parts of Northwest Montana the evening of the 5th into the morning of the 6th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: General snow amounts of 2 to 4 inches with isolated higher totals were observed. The snow combined with much colder temperatures and wind gust of 30 to 40 mph lead to hazardous driving conditions and numerous accidents.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A powerful shortwave trough combined with a moderately unstable air mass, producing showers and thunderstorms across the region. A few of these became severe in the evening, especially as the trough interacted with areas of higher dewpoints close to the North Dakota and Saskatchewan borders. The most significant storms were the supercells which impacted Sheridan County, bringing significant damage to the Plentywood area in the form of a macroburst and an EF-1 tornado.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Director of the North Dakota Agricultural Weather Network (NDAWN) reported through social media that the agrimet mesonet site Dagmar 7WSW measured wind gusts of at least 65 mph from 6:45 PM MST to 7:05 PM MST. The peak wind gust of 86 mph was recorded at 7:00 PM MST.  All times are estimated.

A strong area of high pressure over the Great Basin/Rocky Mountain region and a low pressure system dropping south and east into Saskatchewan brought a strong cold front across central and eastern Montana. Very hot temperatures were in place ahead of the front - reaching the upper 90s and low 100s. Dewpoints were in the 60s and 70s ahead of the front. All of this, combined with strong upper-level winds, provided plenty of instability for severe thunderstorms. By late afternoon, storms began to form and move across northeast Montana with 60 to 75 mph wind gusts. This system moved into North Dakota by approximately 10 PM MDT.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotter reported damage to a large rodeo arena and some outbuildings blown down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Flooding was observed on the Clark Fork River as it passes though Missoula beginning on April 29th and around the Seeley Lake area beginning on April 28th. Flooding in both areas continued into May 2018.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Increasing water levels in Seeley Lake, as well as a rising water table lead to flooding of 15 to 20 homes. Most of the flooding occurred on the southwest side of Seeley Lake near C Street where water had surrounded 10 to 15 homes.  Several homes in the town of Seeley Lake also had groundwater-filled basements.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Arctic air spilled over the Continental Divide once again leading to strong winds and snow which made travel difficult in parts of Northwest Montana the evening of the 5th into the morning of the 6th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An unseasonably late winter storm brought snow amounts of 2 to 4 inches with isolated higher totals observed. The snow combined with wind gust of 35 to 45 mph lead to reduced visibility and ice, the result was widespread hazardous driving conditions and several traffic accidents.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Flooding was observed on the Clark Fork River as it passes though Missoula beginning on April 29th and around the Seeley Lake area beginning on April 28th. Flooding in both areas continued into May 2018.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Increasing river levels and a rising ground water table resulted in flooding around ten homes in the Orchard Homes subdivision of Missoula. The main impacts observed were water covering parts of Tower Street and Kehrwald Drive with adjacent homes being surrounded by water. Some of the affected homes also had basements fill with water. The full details including damages are in the May report.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The town of Plentywood was directly hit by a macroburst with estimated wind speeds of up to 118 mph. The most significant damage occurred at the Plentywood airport, where the metal beacon tower was bent over to the ground, ten general aviation aircraft were damaged or destroyed, eight hangars were totaled, and 6 other hangars sustained minor to moderate damage.  Widespread tree and roof damage was reported throughout the town, along with a couple of outbuildings and garages destroyed.

An unstable northwest flow aloft resulted in the development of a few severe thunderstorms across portions of the Billings Forecast Area. Large hail along with strong winds brought numerous reports of damage to cars on the west end of Billings. The storm dropped 1.11 inches of rain in 30 minutes at the National Weather Service Office on the west side of town, with the airport only receiving 0.33 inches. The hail ranged in size from pea to golf ball with the golf ball size falling across a narrow swath on the west end of town. The copious amounts of hail, mainly small, combined with the heavy rain, clogged storm drains across portions of the city. This resulted in numerous intersections and parking lots flooding with at least a foot of water.

Through most of the month of September, the overall weather pattern shifted to favor more precipitation for northern Valley and northern Phillips Counties. Enough periods of rain showers occurred to ease drought concerns and to remove Severe Drought Conditions (D-2) by the last week of September.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Drought conditions (D2- Severe) continued through at least the first few weeks of September across the northwestern corner of northern Valley County. A shift in the weather pattern brought more frequent periods of rain showers to the area, especially in the 3rd week of September. For the month, this area received three to four inches of rain, which was two to three inches above normal for the most heavily impacted areas.  Drought conditions were officially down-graded to D1-Moderate on Thursday, September 27th.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotter measured a 68 mph wind gust.

Very hot temperatures were in place ahead of the front - reaching the upper 90s and low 100s. Dewpoints were in the 60s and 70s ahead of the front. All of this, combined with strong upper-level winds, provided plenty of instability for severe thunderstorms. By late afternoon, storms began to form and move across northeast Montana with 60 to 75 mph wind gusts. This system moved into North Dakota by approximately 10 PM MDT.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mesonet site Lustre 8SSW measured a 60 mph wind gust.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper-level shortwave trough moved from the Pacific Northwest and northern California to central Montana and western Wyoming from the early evening of March 3rd through late evening of March 4th. At 700 mb, a low- to mid-level shortwave trough shifted from Idaho and vicinity to eastern Montana. Simultaneously, a low pressure center developed within this shortwave. Horizontal wind shear associated with the counter-clockwise, inward circulation of this low led to weak 850-700 mb frontogenesis and associated ascent over North-Central and Southwest Montana. Large-scale ascent associated with the aforementioned frontogenesis and upper-level divergence ahead of the shortwave trough, combined with sufficient moisture, led to the development of snow over the region. At the surface, a front meandered near the Rocky Mountain Front and the mountains near Great Falls. This front separated cold air to its north from relatively-milder air to its south. This system delivered significant snow amounts to portions of North-Central and Southwest Montana, while the heaviest snow was focused east of I-15. While snow fell March 3rd through 5th, the bulk of accumulation occurred on the 3rd and 4th. In addition, Great Falls set a new snowfall record for March 4th (5.1 inches). The previous record was 4.0 inches set in 1918.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public report of 9-inches of new snow since midnight March 4th. Report location was 5-miles E of Gallatin Gateway at elevation of nearly 5335 feet MSL.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Southwest flow ahead of an upper level trough produced favorable conditions for severe thunderstorms generally across southwest Montana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: This storm produced enough hail to cover the ground several inches in the Hamilton area. There were multiple reports of one inch hail. A lady reported that she would have to replace her roof due to the hail damage. She remarked that she knew of several others who had to get their roofs repaired.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms developed over and near the higher terrain of north-central and southwest Montana during the afternoon and early evening of Thursday, May 17th as a low pressure center aloft exited northeastern Montana to the east and a shortwave trough approached Montana from southwestern Canada. One thunderstorm managed to produce a downburst several miles south of Lewistown. Also, a supercell moving eastward from the Big Snowy Mountains produced quarter-size hail over the plains south of Grass Range.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Fergus County Emergency Manager relayed a rancher's report of a downburst 10-miles south of Lewistown. The downburst damaged several cottonwood trees. In addition, the storm produced pea-size hail and over 2-inches of rainfall in only 30-minutes. The time and location of this event were estimated via radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moved eastward and encountered weak to moderate instability and moderate vertical wind shear over the area. Accordingly, scattered showers and thunderstorms developed along and ahead of the front during the afternoon and early evening of May 26th, with some of this convection developing via daytime heating of the higher terrain. Several thunderstorms became severe and produced quarter-size hail over Jefferson and northern Lewis & Clark Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: KRTV relayed report and pictures of quarter-size hail 1-mile SE of Augusta. Time of report was estimated via radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A frontal boundary had stalled from near Bozeman to near Lewistown for most of May 31st. However, this front began moving southeastward as a cold front by early evening that day. Isentropic lift along the front and daytime heating of higher terrain led to the development of showers and thunderstorms during the afternoon and early evening. Weak to moderate instability and strong southwesterly vertical wind shear enabled some storms to become strong to severe. One thunderstorm cluster produced quarter-size hail in central Lewis & Clark County as the storms moved northeastward.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Spotter reported quarter-size hail 1-mile NNW of Canyon Creek as a cluster of thunderstorms moved through.
