Above normal snowpack combined with a warmer than normal month of May contributed to flooding across western Montana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Bitterroot River near Missoula flooded again and this time reached 12 feet which caused some flooding of farmland and some roads along the river from Lolo to the confluence with the Clark Fork River. Groundwater adjacent to the Bitterroot River flooded Linda Vista Golf course and nearby homeowner's backyards.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several severe thunderstorms moved across the Billings Forecast Area during the late afternoon into the evening hours of the 31st. The most notable severe thunderstorm moved across the Roundup area. This thunderstorm produced very large hail along with flash flooding. A damage survey was conducted which found that a majority of homes and businesses within Roundup experienced some type of hail damage. Damage was to the north and west facing aspects of homes and businesses with numerous broken windows and pitted siding and fencing. Six county buildings along with the hospital and high school were damaged.  Approximately 40 to 50 percent of the local businesses were damaged.  In addition, 100 percent of vehicles and campers left in the open were damaged with dents or windshields broken. Trees were stripped of leaves. ||Flooding also occurred across town and portions of the surrounding area. Highway 87 had several areas where debris had washed over the road from floodwaters. Main Street through Roundup was similar, with low lying areas (in and around the Busy Bee Cafe) being flooded. In addition, Bender Road was washed out between Log Cabin Road and County Road #4. The peak river stage of the Musselshell River at Roundup was around 11.5 feet, a foot and a half above flood stage and the sixth highest crest on record. One home was evacuated due to being surrounded by floodwaters.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public reported flash flooding along Fish Creek about 14 miles south of Shawmut. Low lying areas and creeks flooded with some roads being impassable.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Through most of the month of September, the overall weather pattern shifted to favor more precipitation for northern Valley and northern Phillips Counties. Enough periods of rain showers occurred to ease drought concerns and to remove Severe Drought Conditions (D-2) by the last week of September.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Drought conditions (D2- Severe) continued through at least the first few weeks of September across the northern edge and northeastern corner of northern Phillips County. A shift in the weather pattern brought more frequent periods of rain showers to the area, especially in the 3rd week of September. For the month, this area received two to four inches of rain, which was one to two inches above normal for the most heavily impacted areas.  Drought conditions were officially down-graded to D1-Moderate on Thursday, September 27th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Rivers across western Montana exceeded flood stage during much of May 2018. Flooding caused damage of various levels to homes in the Missoula and Seeley Lake regions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Clark Fork River north of Missoula reached 13.82 feet and 32,500 CFS on May 11 between 830 pm and 845 pm MDT which goes down as the highest river level since June 1, 1908 when the river reached 17.4 feet. Three mobile homes were washed away and most likely broke up off of Schmidt Road on May 11. Multiple residences and structures in the Orchard Homes area, including those along Tower Street and Kehrwald Drive experienced flood damage. Flooding caused the evacuation of 50 to 60 homes conducted by the Missoula County Sheriff and Northwest Energy had to cut electricity and gas to these homes. Despite the fact that this continuous flooding ended on June 8, the event altered the river channel to the extent flooding occurred well below the established flood stage on June 11 and again with a basin rain event on June 19-25.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Above normal snowpack combined with a warmer than normal month of May contributed to flooding across western Montana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Surface water from the Clark Fork River flooded the county road that accesses the sewage lagoons in Plains. The US Army Corps of Engineers had to construct levees to protect the sewage lagoons and they had to rep-rap county roads along the river to prevent roads from being washed out.

Six county buildings along with the hospital and high school were damaged.  Approximately 40 to 50 percent of the local businesses were damaged.  In addition, 100 percent of vehicles and campers left in the open were damaged with dents or windshields broken. Trees were stripped of leaves. ||Flooding also occurred across town and portions of the surrounding area. Highway 87 had several areas where debris had washed over the road from floodwaters. Main Street through Roundup was similar, with low lying areas (in and around the Busy Bee Cafe) being flooded. In addition, Bender Road was washed out between Log Cabin Road and County Road #4. The peak river stage of the Musselshell River at Roundup was around 11.5 feet, a foot and a half above flood stage and the sixth highest crest on record. One home was evacuated due to being surrounded by floodwaters.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Spring-time snowmelt flooding from the previous month continued into May. Most locations along the Milk River dropped below flood stage over the first week of May, and the river levels along the Musselshell and Yellowstone Rivers had already dropped below flood stage in late April.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Milk River at Nashua (NSHM8) began flooding on April 25th due to excessive snowmelt. This flooding continued into May, cresting at 22.35 feet at 2:15 PM MST on April 30th, with a second crest of 22.33 feet on May 1st at 4:15 AM MST. The river levels then began to drop, finally dropping below flood stage on May 6th at 9:30 AM MST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Spring-time snowmelt flooding from the previous month continued into May. EVENT_NARRATIVE: Excessive snowmelt brought the Milk River at Glasgow (GLWM8) into flood stage on April 18th, cresting on April 29th at a level of 30.09 feet. River levels began to drop afterwards, but flooding continued until river levels dropped below flood stage on May 7th at 2:00 AM MST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Multiple thunderstorms developed over north-central and southwest Montana during the afternoon and evening of May 9th, 2018 as a shortwave trough and accompanying Pacific cold front approached from the west. An ordinary cell thunderstorm produced a brief tornado six-miles southwest of Moore, MT in the early evening. Multiple pictures indicated the tornado lofted dirt into the air, but no damage, injuries, or deaths occurred. Also, a flash flood occurred in Helena later that evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: KRTV received multiple photos of a tornado six-miles southwest of Moore, MT. Tornado moved southward and lasted about five-minutes per observer. Path length and maximum path width were estimated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As a low pressure center aloft consolidated over Washington state, isolated showers and thunderstorms developed over north-central and southwest Montana during the afternoon and early evening of May 10th, 2018. This was especially the case over and near higher terrain. One thunderstorm near Havre managed to generate severe wind gusts as the storm collapsed. Lastly, the combination of rainfall and melting snow led to minor flooding in Jefferson County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Jefferson County Disaster and Emergency Services (DES) reported water flowing across Hubbard Lane, near Boulder. Flooding was believed to have resulted from a combination of rain and snow melt.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Satellite imagery reveals a strong upper level jet. A region of strong flow aloft moves eastward into the region, causing widespread strong winds across much of central MT. Strong wind gusts develop along the immediate east slopes of the Rocky Mtn front as the initial surge of strong west flow aloft arrives in combination with favorable temperature/wind profiles for Mtn Wave/downslope wind enhancement. Strong west flow aloft overspreads much of north-central MT with gusts peaking during the evening hours. Strongest winds for eastern and southern portions of central MT most likely to occur with the passage of the associated Pacific surface cold front/trough during the evening time frame.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High wind gust of 73 mph measured at mesonet 14 W Bynum.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Hillsboro RAWS recorded a wind gust of 60 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An isolated thunderstorm produced some wind damage in Miles City.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A camper was damaged by a large tree branch that snapped.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front extending from a surface low over Saskatchewan, combined with a strong low pressure trough over northeast Montana amid a very unstable atmosphere to produce numerous thunderstorms, some of which became severe. Later in the evening, some of these storms merged together, produced strong winds as they departed Montana. In addition, heavy rain, caused a few roads to wash out.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Via social media, public reported golf ball size hail.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind gust of 71 MPH measured at mesonet station mtbwg Browning.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: With a weakly unstable atmosphere, strong thunderstorms from the higher terrain of central and southern Montana eventually developed into a few isolated severe thunderstorms and pushed strong to severe wind gusts into portions of northeast Montana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: NWS employee estimated 60 mph wind gusts.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Significant runoff from periods of rain and melting of a deep mountain snowpack allowed flooding to begin in the Lincoln area on April 29th and persist into May. Floodwaters finally receded on May 27th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lincoln Volunteer Fire Chief reported flooding in subdivision along Stonewall Creek just north-northwest of Lincoln. A debris flow from the Park Creek Fire's burn scar entered the creek and diverted the streamflow, leading to flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Warm, moist air combined with favorable winds and an approaching low pressure system from the west to form a number of strong thunderstorm across portions of northeast Montana - one of which became severe just before crossing the border into North Dakota.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Local fire department officials were responding to a vehicle accident just barely on the Montana side of the border, along Road 327, and reported quarter size hail stones.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moved eastward and encountered weak to moderate instability and moderate vertical wind shear over the area. Accordingly, scattered showers and thunderstorms developed along and ahead of the front during the afternoon and early evening of May 26th, with some of this convection developing via daytime heating of the higher terrain. Several thunderstorms became severe and produced quarter-size hail over Jefferson and northern Lewis & Clark Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Spotter reported quarter-size hail 2-miles ESE of Cardwell as a supercell moved overhead.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moved eastward and encountered weak to moderate instability and moderate vertical wind shear over the area. EVENT_NARRATIVE: Spotter reported quarter-size hail 5-miles ESE of Cardwell as a second supercell moved overhead.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Disturbances in the moist and unstable southwest flow aloft spread scattered showers and thunderstorms through the area.  Warmer than normal temperatures and dry conditions near the surface caused gusty and erratic winds in association with these storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mesonet Station EKHM8, Elkhorn RAWS - 8 miles west of Townsend. 66 mph thunderstorm wind gust.  Time of event is estimated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Multiple subtle disturbances embedded in west-southwesterly flow aloft, along with primarily weak instability and moderate vertical wind shear, resulted in thunderstorm activity and convective high winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind Gusts of 63 MPH at Fresno Reservoir.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Multiple subtle disturbances embedded in west-southwesterly flow aloft, along with primarily weak instability and moderate vertical wind shear, resulted in thunderstorm activity and convective high winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind gust of 59 mph along with brief heavy rain.  Rainfall of 0.30.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak storm system combined with a rather dry atmosphere to produce strong to severe wind gusts across central and eastern Montana, resulting in damage to portions of Prairie County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Prairie County dispatch reported that high winds damaged a power line, starting a small grass fire.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On August 2nd, isolated to scattered showers and thunderstorms developed over central and southwest MT, along and ahead of a shortwave trough and cold front approaching from the west. A couple of storms managed to produce marginally-severe wind gusts as precipitation transported stronger winds aloft down to the surface.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Antelope RAWS, about five-miles NE of Lima, reported 58-mph peak gust from thunderstorm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On August 2nd, isolated to scattered showers and thunderstorms developed over central and southwest MT, along and ahead of a shortwave trough and cold front approaching from the west. A couple of storms managed to produce marginally-severe wind gusts as precipitation transported stronger winds aloft down to the surface.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lewistown Airport ASOS reported 58-mph peak gust from thunderstorm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Showers and thunderstorms developed via daytime heating of higher terrain in Southwest MT before moving northeastward. Sufficiently-strong vertical wind shear contributed to several instances of severe wind gusts as these storms affected North-Central MT.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong winds knocked down some tree branches up to 33 inches in length.  Estimated wind gust 58 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Virga managed to produce a dry downburst in Vaughn, near Great Falls, during the early afternoon of August 3rd. At 12:44 PM MST, personnel in the control tower at Great Falls International Airport reported a dust plume associated with the outflow to the north of the airport. They estimated the dust extended to 300 feet AGL.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Spotter estimated a peak gust of 60-mph from virga.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Showers and thunderstorms developed via daytime heating of higher terrain in Southwest MT before moving northeastward. Sufficiently-strong vertical wind shear contributed to several instances of severe wind gusts as these storms affected North-Central MT.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Large healthy conifer tree downed at the roots in high winds from thunderstorm. Estimated peak gust of 60 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The prevailing weather pattern favored a high pressure ridge over the region for many weeks. A persistent lack of rainfall for northern Phillips and northern Valley Counties led to small areas of Severe Drought Conditions (D-2) which lasted throughout the month of August.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rainfall amounts from the previous few months were around one half to one inch below normal across the northern edge and northeastern corner of northern Phillips County. After the first week of August, as the vegetation dried out, drought conditions (D2 - Severe) began there by August 7th. The latter part of the month provided some relief with one to two inches of precipitation, which was just below normal for August across the most heavily impacted areas. As recovery was still far away, drought conditions persisted into September.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rainfall amounts from the previous few months were around one to two inches below normal across the northwestern corner of northern Valley County. The latter part of the month provided some relief with one to two inches of precipitation, which was approximately near normal for August across the most heavily impacted areas. As recovery was still far away, drought conditions persisted into September.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stalled ridge of strong high pressure over the region allowed high temperatures to climb well into the triple digits for many locations for multiple days.  Relative humidity values increased enough to make conditions unusually humid. These conditions combined to warrant the issuance of this area's first excessive heat warning.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: During the afternoon hours of the 11th and 12th at the Glendive airport, high temperatures ranged from 102 to 106 degrees while dew points ranged from the mid 40s to mid 50s.  Even though this resulted in relative humidity values dropping down into the teens, the unusually hot temperatures and stagnant winds added stress to those with heat sensitivities.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Some wind damage occurred on Bench Boulevard. A large cottonwood tree split in half and fell on a fence causing damage to the fence.  A dead pine also fell with the root ball being exposed. The strong winds occurred from convection aloft but no lightning was detected.

Scattered showers and thunderstorms developed over southwest Montana during the afternoon of August 20th and continued into the evening. This activity occurred within a weakly to moderately-unstable air mass amidst moderate vertical wind shear ahead of a Canadian cold front. A brief tornado formed within an eastward-moving squall line as it traversed the Wisdom area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Retired geologist observed a tornado on his property about 1-mile ESE of Wisdom. 50-lb. deck table was blown about 10 feet, a small greenhouse was leveled, and portions of fences were damaged. His personal weather station recorded a 78-mph peak gust at 16:38 MST. In addition, he estimated the tornado moved eastward at about 25 mph for about 3/4 mile. Thus, it is estimated that the tornado occurred between 16:38 and 16:40 MST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow-moving storm system through southern Montana combined with a moderately unstable atmosphere to produce multiple thunderstorms across eastern Montana during the morning hours of August 23rd. One of these stronger storms became severe and a period of heavy rain lead to flash flooding in Prairie County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public reported hail up to the size of quarters and heavy rain.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A ridge of high pressure brought a record breaking heat wave across lower elevations in northwest Montana August 9th and 10th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thompson Falls tied an all-time record high of 107 on the 10th and reached 106 on the 9th. The highest two day mean high temperature ever for Thompson Falls occurred on the 9th and the 10th with observations going back to 1956. Superior reached a record high of 105 on the 9th and 107 on the 10th, ranking as the third hottest two day maximum mean temperature stretch with observations going back to 1914.  Heron also set a record high of 102 on the 10th. Even though near-record to record high temperatures were observed on the 9th and 10th, overnight temperatures dropped in the mid to upper 50s at night.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow-moving storm system through southern Montana combined with a moderately unstable atmosphere to produce multiple thunderstorms across eastern Montana during the morning hours of August 23rd. EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public reported quarter size hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow-moving storm system through southern Montana combined with a moderately unstable atmosphere to produce multiple thunderstorms across eastern Montana during the morning hours of August 23rd. One of these stronger storms became severe and a period of heavy rain lead to flash flooding in Prairie County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain began falling at approximately 4:30 AM MST. Public reported water swiftly flowing across Cabin Creek Road and flooding the yard of a home in the area. Total rainfall amount estimated in excess of 4 inches by 9:00 AM MST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow-moving storm system through southern Montana combined with a moderately unstable atmosphere to produce multiple thunderstorms across eastern Montana during the morning hours of August 23rd. One of these stronger storms became severe and a period of heavy rain lead to flash flooding in Prairie County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public reported water running over a roadway, displacing some of the gravel. Location is on Cabin Creek Road, south of Ash Creek Road. Time estimated by radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A ridge of high pressure brought a record breaking heat wave across lower elevations in northwest Montana August 9th and 10th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Missoula reached a record high of 104 on the 10th. Hamilton and Stevensville recorded near record highs of 97 on the 10th. Even with record to near record highs on the 10th, overnight lows on the 9th and 10th dropped in the low to mid 50s.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A ridge of high pressure brought a record breaking heat wave across lower elevations in northwest Montana August 9th and 10th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Seeley Lake reached a record high of 98 on the 10th and tied a record high of 95 on the 9th. Potomac also set a record high of 100 on the 10th. Low temperatures on the 10th dropped into the low to mid 40s.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A ridge of high pressure brought a record breaking heat wave across lower elevations in northwest Montana August 9th and 10th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Libby Dam recorded an all-time high temperature of 108 on the 10th with observations going back to 1986. In addition, a mean two day maximum temperature of 104.5 on the 9th and 10th was the hottest on record. Eureka set all-time record highs of 106 on the 9th and 10th.  The maximum two day mean of 106 is 1.5 degrees warmer than the next hottest two day stretch with observations going back to 1960. Troy also set a record high of 106 on the 10th. Even though record or near record highs were observed on the 9th and 10th, overnight lows still dropped into the mid 50s to low 60s.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A ridge of high pressure brought a record breaking heat wave across lower elevations in northwest Montana August 9th and 10th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: West Glacier set an all-time record high of 100 on the August 10th. The two day mean high of 99.5 on the 9th and 10th was the hottest on record going back to 1948. Hungry Horse Dam also set a record high on the 10th of 102. Swan Lake reached 99 on the 9th and set an all-time record high of 106 on the 10th. This two day stretch tied the all-time hottest two day mean maximum temperature stretch with observations going back to 1963. Even with near record to record high temperatures lows were able to drop into the low 50s to upper 50s.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A ridge of high pressure brought a record breaking heat wave across lower elevations in northwest Montana August 9th and 10th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Kalispell reached a record high of 100 on August 10th. Creston also record a record high of 98 on August 10th. The Hot Springs RAWS reached 107 and the Jette RAWS reached 103 on the 10th; however, RAWS sites tend to display a warm bias.  Low temperatures dropped into the low to mid 50s on the morning of the 10th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very dry atmosphere ahead of a cold front, combined with a line of rain showers from the west to produce isolated severe downburst winds in northwestern Garfield County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: South Sawmill Creek RAWS site measured a 66 mph wind gust.  Time estimated by radar.  Best guess is that this resulted from a downburst from high based rain clouds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A Pacific cold front swept eastward through north-central Montana during the afternoon and evening of 9 June 2018. This front encountered a warm, relatively-humid, and unstable air mass amidst moderate to strong vertical wind shear. Accordingly, scattered showers and thunderstorms, including supercells, developed. Some of these storms became severe.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Spotter reported ping pong ball-size hail 2-miles NW of Benchland.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms developed along and ahead of a Pacific cold front and shortwave trough during the afternoon and evening of June 21st. Moderate instability and vertical wind shear allowed multiple storms to become severe, resulting in numerous reports of severe wind gusts and several instances of severe hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mesonet station in Ennis recorded 65-mph peak gust from thunderstorm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A trough aloft and embedded low pressure center moved slowly east-southeastward over north-central and southwest Montana on June 23rd. Abundant low-level moisture and cold air aloft associated with the trough allowed scattered showers and thunderstorms to develop via daytime heating alone. A nearly-stationary cluster of showers and thunderstorms impacted the Shelby area from late morning through early evening, resulting in excessive rainfall. The Teton River also flooded in Loma. Elsewhere, a non-mesocyclonic tornado touched down near Highwood during the late afternoon. This tornado occurred within a nearly-stationary line of showers and thunderstorms which apparently developed along a low-level convergence boundary. This same line of showers and thunderstorms generated flash flooding in the Bootlegger area, just north-northwest of Great Falls.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Facebook video of flash flooding at and near the US-2/I-15 interchange in Shelby. Some cars stalled in the floodwater.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered showers and thunderstorms developed during the afternoon and early evening hours of June 4th. Some of this activity formed via daytime heating of the higher terrain, while other showers and storms were triggered by a cold front approaching from the west. Two brief supercells managed to produce quarter-size hail over Judith Basin and Fergus Counties, respectively.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public reported hail 1-mile NW of Coffee Creek. Mainly dime to nickel-size, but a few hailstones were quarter-size.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Moderate instability, moderate vertical wind shear, and unusually-high humidity resided over eastern portions of north-central Montana during the late morning and early afternoon of June 7th. A mesoscale convective system moved northward over Fergus County during the late morning as multiple shortwave troughs traversed north-central and southwest Montana. These thunderstorms produced torrential rainfall and hail, with one storm accompanied by hail up to the size of quarters. In addition, the heavy rainfall generated flash flooding in Lewistown.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Spotter reported dime to quarter-size hail, which lasted 20-minutes.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A supercell produced a wet microburst in the vicinity of Simms, Sun River, Fort Shaw, Fairfield, and Power in Cascade and Teton Counties between 6:40 PM and 7:10 PM MDT on June 25th. Winds associated with this microburst were estimated to have been in the range of 70 to 90 mph. Torrential rainfall and several inches of hail accumulation from pea-size to dime-size hailstones also accompanied the storm and caused extensive crop damage. In addition, a line of thunderstorms crossed Great Falls and caused minor street flooding. These thunderstorms were triggered by a Pacific cold front that encountered a warm, humid air mass and moderate instability amidst strong vertical wind shear.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: NWS storm damage survey estimated 75 mph wind gusts broke one to three-inch diameter softwood tree branches near Sun River.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cut-off low pressure system brought 1 to 3 inches of rain to the upper part of the Clark Fork River Basin resulting in significant stream and river rises. High elevation snow melt also added to the water flow issues.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Extensive field flooding occurred along the Clark Fork River between Drummond and Garrison Junction. Jens Frontage Road along Interstate 90 had water flowing over it in several places.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cut-off low pressure system brought 1 to 3 inches of rain to the upper part of the Clark Fork River Basin resulting in significant stream and river rises. High elevation snow melt also added to the water flow issues.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Cottonwood Creek overflowed its banks in downtown Deer Lodge on the afternoon of June 19. The excessive runoff flooded basements and surrounded numerous homes as water covered a whole city block. The Clark Street Bridge was over topped and remained closed for over a day.  In addition, Tin Cup Joe Creek and Fred Burr Creek also experienced minor flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: After flooding much of the month of May 2018, the Clark Fork River continued to exceeded flood stage in early June 2018.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple residences and structures in the Orchard Homes area, including those along Tower Street and Kehrwald Drive experienced flood damage. Flooding began the end of April 2018 and continued through June 12.  The event altered the river channel to the extent flooding occurred well below the established flood stage all way through June 12 and again with a basin rain event on June 18-26.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cut-off low pressure system brought 1 to 3 inches of rain to the upper part of the Clark Fork River Basin resulting in significant stream and river rises. High elevation snow melt also added to the water flow issues.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flooding returned to the Orchard Homes area, primarily on Tower Street and Kehrwald Drive with water into homes, travel was also impacted. An important note is the previously established flood stage was much too high due to changes in channeling from the earlier flooding late April into early June.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow-moving low pressure system accompanied by unusually-large amounts of moisture affected Montana on the 16th through 19th of June. This system resulted in widespread steady to heavy rainfall across southwest and north-central Montana. The highest rain totals were concentrated along the Rocky Mountain Front, where up to about 8-inches of storm-total rainfall were recorded and resulted from northeasterly to easterly low-level upslope flow enhancing precipitation. Numerous rivers flooded and Rogers Pass was closed for a time due to excessive runoff and a debris flow. Flooding impacts lingered for several days beyond the 19th, including flooding along the Sun River, as it took time for runoff to travel downstream.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Spotter reported flood water was still flowing across Rogers Pass and the pass remained closed. Excessive runoff from significant rainfall on June 16th through 19th and debris from a recent wildfire burn scar clogged culverts along the pass, leading to erosion and unstable road conditions.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered showers and thunderstorms developed during the afternoon and early evening hours of June 4th. Some of this activity formed via daytime heating of the higher terrain, while other showers and storms were triggered by a cold front approaching from the west. Two brief supercells managed to produce quarter-size hail over Judith Basin and Fergus Counties, respectively.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm chaser estimated quarter-size hail in Geyser. Vehicle had minor hail damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An influx of mid-level moisture from the southwest and low-level moisture from the southeast allowed showers and thunderstorms to develop via daytime heating of higher terrain amidst moderate to strong vertical wind shear. These showers and storms affected southwest and north-central Montana during the afternoon and early evening of June 6th. As supercells split near the Judith Basin/Fergus County line, the left-moving supercell managed to produce hail up to ping pong ball size in northwestern Fergus County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Spotter reported quarter-size hail and rainwater running in streets in Coffee Creek.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A Pacific cold front swept eastward through north-central Montana during the afternoon and evening of 9 June 2018. This front encountered a warm, relatively-humid, and unstable air mass amidst moderate to strong vertical wind shear. Accordingly, scattered showers and thunderstorms, including supercells, developed. Some of these storms became severe.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: MT DOT sensor 6-miles NNW of Sunburst reported 62-mph peak gust from thunderstorm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Tight pressure gradients across the area resulted in some high wind gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Big Timber recorded a wind gust of 58 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Tight pressure gradients across the area resulted in some high wind gusts.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An active pattern developed across the area with Pacific moisture moving into the region and northwest flow aloft overrunning a strong cold front. Widespread areas of northeast Montana received several inches of snow from this storm system.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Up to 9 inches of snow fell across the area from the night of the 7th and through the day on the 8th. Roads across the area, including Highway 200, were ice and snow covered with significant drifts.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong pressure gradient along the Rocky Mountain Front combined with diurnal mixing created the conditions for strong down sloping winds east of the Continental Divide.  The resulted in dangerous cross winds along north/south running roadways.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: MT DOT reported a semi and trailer were blown over on highway 464 near Housman Hill north of Browning.  Estimated wind gusts were 80 mph at the time of the event.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Runoff from mountain snowmelt and periods of rain renewed flooding along Tenmile Creek in the Helena area by early May. This flooding finally receded by May 19th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Helena Independent Record reported flooding at Rossiter Elementary School and the Helena Campground and RV Park around 2 PM MDT on Sunday, May 6th. Runoff from melting mountain snowpack and recent rainfall caused flooding along Tenmile Creek and nearby irrigation channels.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong, competing weather systems acted to enhance the wind flow between them, directly through central Montana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At the Malta South US-191 DOT site, sustained winds of 40 to 50 mph were persistent between 11:48 AM and 11:45 PM. Peak wind gusts of 71 mph were recorded between 8:15 PM and 8:19 PM.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong winds aloft were able to easily filter down to the surface as the inversion dispersed and a brief disturbance moved through the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At the Malta South US-191 DOT site, even though the maximum wind gust peaked at only 57 mph multiple times, sustained winds of 40 mph or greater were recorded each hour between 11:31 PM on the 18th and 1:45 AM on the 19th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Significant runoff from periods of rain and melting of a deep mountain snowpack allowed flooding to begin in the Lincoln area on April 29th and persist into May. Floodwaters finally receded on May 27th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lincoln Volunteer Fire Chief reported part of Stemple Pass Road southeast of Lincoln was washed out by floodwaters.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Upper level ridge moving into the Northern Rockies  will begin to flatten out as a potent open wave digs southeastward from the Gulf of Alaska and into the Canadian Rockies. At the surface, a lee-side surface trough sharpens rapidly as the surface low moves southeast from West Central British Columbia and into Central Alberta. This sharpening lee-side surface trough will lead to an increasing pressure gradient along the Rocky |Mountain Front (and eventually along the north facing slopes of the Little Belt Mountains), with values peaking out at between 175-250 microbars/km.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High wind gust of 63 mph measured at mesonet station mtbrn, two medicine bridge mdt.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Just enough surface moisture from recent snow melt was available, with near calm winds, and mostly clear skies, to allow dense fog to develop over a few locations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: With brief periods of mist and fog not quite so dense, coming and going for most of the day, a continuous period of 2 to 3 hours of dense fog set in at the airport in Glasgow. Visibility was down to a quarter of a mile or less.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Just enough surface moisture from recent snow melt was available, with near calm winds, and mostly clear skies, to allow dense fog to develop over a few locations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At the Scobey Airport, dense fog with visibility at a quarter mile or less began at 6:55 PM.  After a 3-hour improvement in visibility later that evening, dense fog returned near midnight and continued until around 4:00 the next morning.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Just enough surface moisture from recent snow melt was available, with near calm winds, and mostly clear skies, to allow dense fog to develop over a few locations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The airport in Malta recorded visibility of a quarter of a mile or less for more than 3 hours during the later morning hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An embedded low pressure disturbance moving through the region included strong winds, some of which, in excess of 55 mph reached down to the surface across central and northeastern Montana through the morning and mid-day hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At the Malta South US-191 DOT site, sustained winds of 40 mph were intermittent and did not last for more than 1 hour at a time.  The peak wind gust of 59 mph was recorded at 11:49 AM.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure trough from the west, allowed stronger winds from above to reach down to the surface amid a chinook effect off the slope of the higher terrain across central portions of eastern Montana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At the Malta South US-191 DOT site, sustained winds of 40 mph or greater were measured each hour between 6:45 AM and 11:56 AM. Wind gusts of 58 mph were recorded both at 9:45 AM and 10:15 AM. The peak wind gust of 60 mph was recorded at 11:15 AM.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure trough from the west, allowed stronger winds from above to reach down to the surface amid a chinook effect off the slope of the higher terrain across central portions of eastern Montana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At the South Sawmill Creek RAWS site, a 58 mph wind gust was measured in the hour preceding 9:08 AM.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Satellite imagery reveals a strong upper level jet. A region of strong flow aloft moves eastward into the region, causing widespread strong winds across much of central MT. Strong wind gusts develop along the immediate east slopes of the Rocky Mtn front as the initial surge of strong west flow aloft arrives in combination with favorable temperature/wind profiles for Mtn Wave/downslope wind enhancement. Strong west flow aloft overspreads much of north-central MT with gusts peaking during the evening hours. Strongest winds for eastern and southern portions of central MT most likely to occur with the passage of the associated Pacific surface cold front/trough during the evening time frame.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High wind gust of 65 mph measured at mesonet station mtbrn, two medicine bridge mdt.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Large-scale ascent along with afternoon heating, with cooling/moistening aloft,  resulted in approximately 2000 J/kg of CAPE with little inhibition (CIN). This combined with some veering with height of winds and approximately 20-30 kt mid-level flow supported organized multicell and some supercell structures.

This combined with some veering with height of winds and approximately 20-30 kt mid-level flow supported organized multicell and some supercell structures.
An isolated thunderstorm produced some severe hail near Roundup and Acton.

During the late afternoon and evening hours on June 28th, severe weather broke out across much of the Billings Forecast Area. The most severe weather occurred across southeast Montana, mainly Carter County where golf ball size and larger hail, straight line winds 70-80 mph and four tornadoes were reported. ||Around 7:17 PM, the first tornado was spotted crossing SR 323 in Carter County about 12 miles north of Albion in the Finger Butte Area. This tornado was spotted by a rancher about a mile to the south. His property sustained straight line wind damage on the south side of the storm, including trees uprooted, a barn roof blown off, a shed destroyed, and a horse trailer flipped over. This wind damage was consistent with 70-80 mph winds. This tornado left no trace along SR 323 and moved through open terrain before lifting. This tornado has been assigned a rating of EF-0 since no damage markers could be observed. Storm chasers following this storm as it pushed east through rural areas of Carter County noticed two additional brief tornado touchdowns. These tornadoes were in extremely rural and open areas with no road access to do damage surveys. ||At approximately 8:06 PM, another tornado touched down just east of Camp Crook Road 4 miles south of Capitol, MT. In its initial phases, this tornado skipped along the ground, as confirmed by chaser video, producing sporadic snapped trees and downed limbs consistent with winds 80-90 mph (EF-0 to EF-1). ||Damage survey and eye witness accounts confirm the tornado initially moved north-north-east along the Little Missouri River. As it approached the river it intensified to 120 mph (EF-2) producing an increasingly wider swath of snapped trees several hundred yards wide. The tornado started to turn more to the east at this time, moving on a northeasterly track as it crossed the Little Missouri and moved across the Montana/South Dakota border.||The tornadoes stayed mostly over rural areas producing limited damage. The damage that has been surveyed has ranged from EF-0 to EF-3. The most significant damage appears to have occurred with a tornado that touched down south of Capitol, Montana and moved into South Dakota. Damaged observed just across the border in South Dakota is consistent with an EF-3 tornado rating, with winds estimated at 136 mph. Damage observed includes one home destroyed, heavy hay bales moved, along with the debarking of some trees.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The tornado was on the ground for 7 miles (approximately 24 minutes) from south of Capitol, Montana northeast into South Dakota. In Montana, the tornado path was 3 miles long with a width of 200 yards. Damage ranged from EF-1 at the initial touchdown, increasing to EF-2 just before the South Dakota border. Large tree limbs were downed and trees snapped. The damage became more widespread as the path approached the Little Missouri River. |The maximum wind speed during the life of the tornado was 136 mph (EF-3). The tornado ranged from 200 yards wide in Montana to a half mile wide in South Dakota.

There was also minor damage to a house as the gutters were ripped off and some of the metal roofing torn off. In addition, numerous large trees were uprooted or snapped in half.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter size hail covered portions of Interstate 94 in the Hysham Hills.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An early morning thunderstorm dumped a quarter to nearly a half inch of rain along with small hail across parts of downtown Billings, as well as the Billings Heights. Flooded streets caused problems for the morning commute as standing water in several intersections caused cars to stall in the deep water at several underpasses.

During the late afternoon and evening hours on June 28th, severe weather broke out across much of the Billings Forecast Area. The most severe weather occurred across southeast Montana, mainly Carter County where golf ball size and larger hail, straight line winds 70-80 mph and four tornadoes were reported. ||Around 7:17 PM, the first tornado was spotted crossing SR 323 in Carter County about 12 miles north of Albion in the Finger Butte Area. This tornado was spotted by a rancher about a mile to the south. His property sustained straight line wind damage on the south side of the storm, including trees uprooted, a barn roof blown off, a shed destroyed, and a horse trailer flipped over. This wind damage was consistent with 70-80 mph winds. This tornado left no trace along SR 323 and moved through open terrain before lifting. This tornado has been assigned a rating of EF-0 since no damage markers could be observed. Storm chasers following this storm as it pushed east through rural areas of Carter County noticed two additional brief tornado touchdowns. These tornadoes were in extremely rural and open areas with no road access to do damage surveys. ||At approximately 8:06 PM, another tornado touched down just east of Camp Crook Road 4 miles south of Capitol, MT. In its initial phases, this tornado skipped along the ground, as confirmed by chaser video, producing sporadic snapped trees and downed limbs consistent with winds 80-90 mph (EF-0 to EF-1). ||Damage survey and eye witness accounts confirm the tornado initially moved north-north-east along the Little Missouri River. As it approached the river it intensified to 120 mph (EF-2) producing an increasingly wider swath of snapped trees several hundred yards wide. The tornado started to turn more to the east at this time, moving on a northeasterly track as it crossed the Little Missouri and moved across the Montana/South Dakota border.||The tornadoes stayed mostly over rural areas producing limited damage. The damage that has been surveyed has ranged from EF-0 to EF-3. The most significant damage appears to have occurred with a tornado that touched down south of Capitol, Montana and moved into South Dakota. Damaged observed just across the border in South Dakota is consistent with an EF-3 tornado rating, with winds estimated at 136 mph. Damage observed includes one home destroyed, heavy hay bales moved, along with the debarking of some trees.

Heavy rain at the end of May combined with a severe thunderstorm that produced flash flooding during the evening of May 31st resulted in high water levels and flooding along the Musselshell River. The river level during the early morning hours of June 1st was 11.5 feet which was 1.5 feet above flood stage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The peak river stage of the Musselshell River at Roundup was 11.5 feet, a foot and a half above flood stage and the sixth highest crest on record. These high water levels moved downstream toward the town of Melstone where flooding was reported along  Highway 12 between Roundup and Melstone.  Bender Road was washed out between Log Cabin Road and County Road #4. In addition, Ragged Point Road was washed out approximately 9 miles up near the CRP Fields.

The most significant damage appears to have occurred with a tornado that touched down south of Capitol, Montana and moved into South Dakota. Damaged observed just across the border in South Dakota is consistent with an EF-3 tornado rating, with winds estimated at 136 mph. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the late afternoon and evening hours on June 28th, severe weather broke out across much of the Billings Forecast Area. The most severe weather occurred across southeast Montana, mainly Carter County where golf ball size and larger hail, straight line winds 70-80 mph and four tornadoes were reported. ||Around 7:17 PM, the first tornado was spotted crossing SR 323 in Carter County about 12 miles north of Albion in the Finger Butte Area. This tornado was spotted by a rancher about a mile to the south. His property sustained straight line wind damage on the south side of the storm, including trees uprooted, a barn roof blown off, a shed destroyed, and a horse trailer flipped over. This wind damage was consistent with 70-80 mph winds. This tornado left no trace along SR 323 and moved through open terrain before lifting. This tornado has been assigned a rating of EF-0 since no damage markers could be observed. Storm chasers following this storm as it pushed east through rural areas of Carter County noticed two additional brief tornado touchdowns. These tornadoes were in extremely rural and open areas with no road access to do damage surveys. ||At approximately 8:06 PM, another tornado touched down just east of Camp Crook Road 4 miles south of Capitol, MT. In its initial phases, this tornado skipped along the ground, as confirmed by chaser video, producing sporadic snapped trees and downed limbs consistent with winds 80-90 mph (EF-0 to EF-1). ||Damage survey and eye witness accounts confirm the tornado initially moved north-north-east along the Little Missouri River. As it approached the river it intensified to 120 mph (EF-2) producing an increasingly wider swath of snapped trees several hundred yards wide. The tornado started to turn more to the east at this time, moving on a northeasterly track as it crossed the Little Missouri and moved across the Montana/South Dakota border.||The tornadoes stayed mostly over rural areas producing limited damage. The damage that has been surveyed has ranged from EF-0 to EF-3. The most significant damage appears to have occurred with a tornado that touched down south of Capitol, Montana and moved into South Dakota. Damaged observed just across the border in South Dakota is consistent with an EF-3 tornado rating, with winds estimated at 136 mph. Damage observed includes one home destroyed, heavy hay bales moved, along with the debarking of some trees.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The tornado was on the ground across rural areas for four miles. There were no reports of damage with this tornado.

This combined with some veering with height of winds and approximately 20-30 kt mid-level flow supported organized multicell and some supercell structures.

A low pressure system brought snowfall to elevations above five thousand feet in the Glacier National Park area in northwest Montana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Between 6 and 8 inches of snow fell at Marias Pass on US-2 and road crews reported a changeover from snow to rain at about 7 miles west of the pass. Conditions were raw and hazardous in the back-country due to the snowfall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A Canadian cold front combined with some Pacific moisture created the conditions for the first major Northern Rockies snow event along with strong winds.  There were reports of snowfall amounts in the range of one to two feet leading to higher elevation road closures.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Glacier National Park reported Going to the Sun Road was closed east of the Continental Divide and Cut Bank Road was also closed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front moved across the area resulting in much colder temperatures for this time of year. This,  combined with a moist easterly upslope flow, resulted in heavy snow for the Red Lodge Foothills.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: There were multiple reports of 10 inches of snow across the area. There were travel impacts on Highway 78 west of Red Lodge as a bus and semi truck got stuck in the snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Intense snow showers developed over an arctic cold front leading to numerous traffic accidents on Interstate 90.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy showers brought 1 to 3 inches of snow along the Interstate 90 corridor throughout the afternoon hours. Snow partially melted and packed on to the roadway as ice when temperatures dropped into the teens behind a cold front.  The presence of abundant ice and reduced visibility led to numerous traffic accidents.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong westerly flow aloft and the passage of an embedded shortwave trough combined with a sharp lee surface trough to generate high surface wind gusts across parts of north-central and southwest Montana on November 2nd. Eventually the lee surface trough shifted eastward with the shortwave trough, allowing gusty winds to progress farther east.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Dellwo mesonet Station (14-miles W of Bynum) recorded a peak gust of 60-mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A shortwave trough embedded in westerly flow aloft traversed north-central and southwest Montana on November 4th. At the surface, a tight pressure gradient accompanied a low pressure center moving eastward across southern Alberta and Saskatchewan. This pattern yielded downslope winds and a few high wind gusts in north-central and southwest Montana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Gross Ranch mesonet station (18-miles SW of Augusta) recorded 61-mph peak gust.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall of 8 to 11 inches, along with slick roads, was reported across the Red Lodge area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Isolated very strong wind gusts were reported in the Bridger area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Bridger Co-op reported damaging winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Tight pressure gradients resulted in high wind gusts across southern Wheatland County.
An upper low over Idaho combined with a moist easterly upslope flow resulted in heavy snow across the Beartooth/Absaroka Mountains and adjacent foothills.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Red Lodge Ski area and Mystic Lake received 12 to 19 inches of snow.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall of 7 to 8 inches was reported in the Dean and Nye areas.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall of 6 to 10 inches was common across the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low stratus and freezing fog developed during the late evening hours of November 20th throughout the valleys of Northwest Montana, and lasted into the morning hours of November 21st. Supercooled water droplets stuck to freezing surfaces causing black ice on sidewalks and roadways resulting in multiple accidents.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Supercooled water droplets from fog stuck to freezing surfaces causing black ice on sidewalks and roadways resulting in multiple accidents.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Lift induced by an approaching trough aloft from the west combined with abundant Pacific moisture streaming into southwest and central Montana to generate periods of snow during the evening of November 23rd into the 24th. The most impactful snow fell in the mountains of southwest Montana, especially on the 24th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Due to slick, snow-covered roads, MT DOT required towing units to have chains on I-90 at Bozeman Pass.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A sharp surface trough in the lee of the Rockies and strong westerly flow aloft generated strong southwesterly to westerly downslope winds along the Rocky Mountain Front and adjacent plains. Several instances of high wind gusts occurred on November 13th and 14th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: MT DOT station (2-miles SE of Browning) recorded peak gust of 60-mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stagnant weather pattern and light winds under a ridge of high pressure allowed some areas of freezing fog to develop and persist over the lower elevations of the Milk, Missouri, and Yellowstone River valleys of northeast Montana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Visibility, due to dense freezing fog began a bit earlier and was more intermittent at the Poplar airport mesonet site; but at the Wolf Point airport ASOS site, dense freezing fog with visibility at a quarter of a mile or less was persistent for about 8 hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stagnant weather pattern and light winds under a ridge of high pressure allowed some areas of freezing fog to develop and persist over the lower elevations of the Milk, Missouri, and Yellowstone River valleys of northeast Montana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The mesonet site at the airport in Scobey recorded 8 continuous hours of dense freezing fog with visibility at a quarter of a mile.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A Canadian cold front moved southward through north-central Montana during the early and mid-morning of Friday, November 16th. This front then cleared southwest Montana between mid-morning and late afternoon. Rain quickly changed to snow following the frontal passage and wet roadways froze rapidly. The combination of ice and snow along roads had a significant impact on travel in some areas, including Great Falls. In addition, strong cold air advection and boundary layer mixing behind the front contributed to gusty northerly winds. Periods of snow continued across north-central and southwest Montana through much of Friday before ending late that evening or during the early morning of Saturday, November 17th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: King Coulee mesonet station (7-miles NW of Devon) recorded peak gust of 58-mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Widespread snow fell across North-central Idaho into western Montana, impacting both valleys and mountain passes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Periods of heavy, wet snow totaling 6 to 12 inches impacted the Interstate 90 corridor leading to slick roads and travel restrictions on Lookout Pass.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An arctic cold front caused gusty winds leading to black ice formation on wet roadways in the Flathead Valley.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Light snow of 1 to 2 inches fell during the early morning hours, then melted on roadways.  Gusty winds to 37 mph at KGPI and below freezing temperatures caused wet roads to ice over with numerous traffic accidents reported especially in the city of Kalispell during this early season event.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm and moist air mass brought a mix of wintry precipitation across western Montana December 29th and 30th. Periods of heavy snowfall were observed as an arctic cold front pushed through western Montana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A period of light freezing rain took place the evening of the 29th ahead of an arctic cold front. The arctic front pushed across the Continental Divide December 30th and a period of heavy snowfall and blowing snow lead to significant reductions in visibility down to a few hundred feet between Essex and Marias Pass. Snowfall between Essex and Marias Pass ranged anywhere from one to two feet along US-2.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm and moist air mass brought a mix of wintry precipitation across western Montana December 29th and 30th. Periods of heavy snowfall were observed as an arctic cold front pushed through western Montana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy snowfall impacted travel on the I-90 corridor and travel restrictions were enforced over Lookout Pass. Periods of heavy snowfall during the morning of December 30th produced gusty winds and visibility less than a quarter mile.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Widespread snow impacted higher elevations of western Montana and north central Idaho, including mountain pass areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy snowfall, with accumulations of two to three feet was observed at Lookout Pass. Driving restrictions were enforced the afternoon of the 11th and severe driving conditions were declared during the early morning hours of the 12th between Lookout Pass and Taft on I-90.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Widespread snow impacted higher elevations of western Montana and north central Idaho, including mountain pass areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A period of heavy snowfall Tuesday evening through Wednesday morning led to snowfall accumulations of up to 14 inches over Lolo Pass and up to 11 inches near Lost Trail Pass.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Slippery road conditions from black ice contributed to a multi-vehicle accident that resulted in two fatalities and three injuries near Superior.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A little bit before 4 am MDT, five semi-tractor trailers and one SUV crashed into each other at the bridge near the Quartz Flat Campground on Interstate 90 due to icy road conditions. As a result, two men in their 30s passed away while three other men were injured. The road conditions were labeled as very icy. The guardrails were damaged so much that the Montana Department of Transportation had to close the eastbound lanes for 20 hours after the incident.
